preparationGuide.about: This curated list is designed for Cognizant GenC and other technical interviews. The questions are of Moderate difficulty (LeetCode Easy/Medium), covering Arrays, Strings, Hashing, Recursion, Sorting, Greedy, and Basic DP. Each entry includes a problem statement, four plausible options, the correct answer, and a detailed reasoning.
preparationGuide.tip: Do not just memorize answers. Focus on understanding the pattern (e.g., Two Pointers, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum) as Cognizant often asks variations of these classic problems.

questions.title: Two Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

options.A: Use nested loops to check every pair (O(n^2)).
options.B: Sort the array and use two pointers (O(n log n)).
options.C: Use a hash map to store complements while iterating (O(n)).
options.D: Use a recursive divide and conquer approach.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The optimal solution uses a hash map (or dictionary). As you iterate, you check if `target - nums[i]` exists in the map. If yes, return the stored index and the current index. This gives O(n) time complexity. Option B is valid if indices are not needed after sorting (or if we handle carefully), but O(n) is optimal. [citation:3]

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
questions.problem_statement: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the i-th day. You want to maximize profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and a different day in the future to sell that stock. Return the maximum profit.

options.A: Buy at the global minimum and sell at the global maximum.
options.B: Use two nested loops to calculate max difference (O(n^2)).
options.C: Track minimum price so far and calculate max profit in one pass (O(n)).
options.D: Use a stack to track increasing price sequences.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The Kadane-like algorithm works here: keep track of the lowest price seen so far, and at each day calculate the profit if sold today. Keep the maximum profit. Complexity O(n) time and O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Valid Parentheses
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if open brackets are closed by the same type and in the correct order.

options.A: Count the number of opening and closing brackets.
options.B: Use a stack: push opening brackets, when encountering a closing bracket, pop the top and check if it matches.
options.C: Replace all matching pairs iteratively until string is empty.
options.D: Use recursion to match innermost brackets first.

questions.detailed_reasoning: A stack is the standard data structure for parsing syntax. If we see an opening bracket, push it. If we see a closing bracket, the top of the stack must be the corresponding opening bracket. If the stack is empty or mismatch, it's invalid. [citation:1][citation:3]

questions.title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm)
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) which has the largest sum and return its sum.

options.A: Sort the array and pick the largest numbers.
options.B: Use a sliding window of fixed size k.
options.C: Use Kadane's Algorithm: maintain current subarray sum, reset if it becomes negative.
options.D: Calculate prefix sums and find the max difference using two pointers.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Kadane's Algorithm works in O(n) time. We keep a `current_sum` and a `max_sum`. For each number, we update `current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num)` and update `max_sum` accordingly. This handles negative numbers gracefully. [citation:3]

questions.title: Contains Duplicate
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and false if every element is distinct.

options.A: Sort the array and check adjacent elements.
options.B: Use a hash set to store seen elements.
options.C: Use two nested loops to compare all pairs.
options.D: Use the sum of the array to check for duplicates.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Using a hash set gives the most efficient average case O(n) time complexity. As we iterate through the array, if an element already exists in the set, return true; otherwise, add it to the set. Option A (sorting) also works in O(n log n) but modifies the array. [citation:3]

questions.title: Product of Array Except Self
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i]. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time and without using the division operation.

options.A: For each index, multiply all other elements (O(n^2)).
options.B: Calculate total product and divide by nums[i] for each index.
options.C: Use prefix products and suffix products in two passes.
options.D: Use a binary indexed tree to calculate range products.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard two-pass approach: first pass calculates the product of all elements to the left of i (prefix). Second pass multiplies it by the product of all elements to the right of i (suffix). This achieves O(n) time and O(1) extra space (excluding output array). [citation:3]

questions.title: Maximum Product Subarray
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, find a contiguous non-empty subarray within the array that has the largest product, and return the product.

options.A: Use the same logic as Maximum Subarray sum (Kadane's Algorithm).
options.B: Track both maximum and minimum product so far because negative numbers can flip the max to min.
options.C: Use a sliding window of variable size.
options.D: Sort the array and multiply the largest k elements.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Unlike sum, the product of a subarray can become negative and later become positive when multiplied by another negative. Therefore, we need to keep track of both `max_so_far` and `min_so_far`. At each step, we compute candidates: nums[i], max_so_far * nums[i], min_so_far * nums[i] and update accordingly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. Given the rotated array, find the minimum element. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(log n) time.

options.A: Iterate through the array to find the drop point (O(n)).
options.B: Use binary search: if mid > right, min is in right half; else min is in left half.
options.C: Perform a linear scan from the beginning until a drop is found.
options.D: Use a stack to track decreasing sequences.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search is required for O(log n). The property of a rotated sorted array is that one half is always sorted. Compare `nums[mid]` with `nums[right]`. If `nums[mid] > nums[right]`, the minimum lies in the right half (mid+1 to right); otherwise, the minimum lies in the left half (left to mid). [citation:3]

questions.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: Given a rotated sorted array and a target value, return the index of target if found, otherwise -1. Your algorithm must run in O(log n) time.

options.A: Find the pivot (minimum element) using binary search, then binary search in the appropriate half.
options.B: Perform linear search until the target is found.
options.C: Use two pointers from both ends and move towards the center.
options.D: Use a hash map to store indices for O(1) lookup.

questions.detailed_reasoning: First, find the rotation pivot (the smallest element) using binary search. This divides the array into two sorted halves. Then, determine which half the target belongs to by comparing with nums[0] and perform binary search on that half. This achieves O(log n). [citation:3]

questions.title: 3Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i, j, k are distinct indices and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.

options.A: Use three nested loops (O(n^3)).
options.B: Sort the array, fix one element, and use two pointers to find the other two.
options.C: Use a hash set to store pairs and check for complements.
options.D: Sort the array and use binary search for the third element.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The optimal solution is O(n^2). Sort the array first. Then iterate i from 0 to n-3. For each i, use two pointers (left = i+1, right = n-1) to find pairs that sum to -nums[i]. Skip duplicates by moving pointers past duplicate values. [citation:3]

questions.title: Container With Most Water
questions.problem_statement: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the i-th line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]). Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water. Return the maximum amount of water.

options.A: Calculate area for every pair of lines (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use two pointers starting from both ends, always moving the pointer with the smaller height inward.
options.C: Sort the heights and use a greedy approach from the largest height.
options.D: Use a stack to find the next greater element.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pointer technique works because the area is limited by the shorter line. By moving the pointer with the smaller height inward, we potentially increase the height while decreasing width, which might yield a larger area. This achieves O(n) time complexity. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

options.A: Generate all substrings and check for duplicates (O(n^3)).
options.B: Use a hash set and expand the window, shrinking when a duplicate is found.
options.C: Use a stack to push characters and pop when duplicates occur.
options.D: Sort the string and find the longest contiguous unique sequence.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The sliding window with a hash set (or hash map for index tracking) works in O(n). Expand the right pointer. If the character at right is in the set, move the left pointer up to remove the duplicate. Track the maximum window size. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.

options.A: Reverse the string and find the longest common substring.
options.B: Use dynamic programming with a 2D table to track palindromes.
options.C: Expand around center: treat each index (and between indexes) as center and expand outward.
options.D: Use Manacher's Algorithm which is the most efficient.

questions.detailed_reasoning: While Manacher's Algorithm (Option D) gives O(n), 'Expand Around Center' is the standard interview answer (O(n^2) time, O(1) space). For each index, we expand for both odd-length (center at i) and even-length (center between i and i+1) palindromes. DP is O(n^2) time and space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Group Anagrams
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.

options.A: Sort each string and use the sorted string as a key in a hash map.
options.B: Compare every pair of strings to check if they are anagrams (O(n^2 * k)).
options.C: Use a frequency count of characters as the key (e.g., '1#2#0#...').
options.D: Use a trie to store all strings and group at leaf nodes.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The simplest and efficient approach is to sort each string (O(k log k)) and use that as a key in a dictionary. All anagrams will produce the same sorted string. Option C is also valid and gives O(n * k) but is slightly more complex to implement. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find All Anagrams in a String
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings s and p, return an array of all the start indices of p's anagrams in s. You may return the answer in any order.

options.A: Generate all substrings of length p.length() and check if they are anagrams.
options.B: Use a fixed-size sliding window with a frequency array for characters.
options.C: Sort p and use two pointers to compare sorted substrings.
options.D: Use a stack to match characters dynamically.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The sliding window with character frequency counts is optimal. Maintain a frequency count of characters in p and a sliding window of the same size in s. Slide the window across s, updating the frequency counts (add new character, remove old character) and compare if the counts match. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. A binary tree's maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.

options.A: Perform BFS level order traversal and count the number of levels.
options.B: Perform DFS recursion: 1 + max(maxDepth(left), maxDepth(right)).
options.C: Count the number of nodes in the tree.
options.D: Use a stack to simulate DFS and track the maximum depth.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Recursive DFS is the simplest O(n) solution. The base case: if root is null, return 0. Otherwise, return 1 + the maximum of the depth of the left and right subtrees. BFS (Option A) also works but uses more code. [citation:8]

questions.title: Invert Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree (swap every left child with the right child) and return the root.

options.A: Perform a BFS traversal and swap children at each node.
options.B: Use recursion: swap the left and right children, then recursively invert the left and right subtrees.
options.C: Use an inorder traversal and swap nodes.
options.D: Perform a preorder traversal and store nodes in a list, then rebuild.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The classic solution uses DFS recursion. For each node, swap its left and right children, then recursively call invert on the left child and right child. BFS (Option A) works too, but recursion is cleaner. [citation:8]

questions.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given a binary search tree (BST) and two nodes p and q, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA). The LCA is defined as the lowest node that has both p and q as descendants.

options.A: Perform a BFS to find paths to p and q, then compare paths.
options.B: Use recursion: if both p and q are less than root, go left; if both greater, go right; otherwise root is LCA.
options.C: Perform a postorder traversal and return when both p and q are found.
options.D: Sort the nodes and use binary search to find the split point.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The BST property allows O(h) time (h is height). If both p.val and q.val are less than root.val, LCA is in the left subtree. If both are greater, LCA is in the right subtree. Otherwise, the current root is the LCA. [citation:8]

questions.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (i.e., from left to right, level by level).

options.A: Perform DFS with recursion and pass the level as a parameter, appending nodes to a list of lists.
options.B: Use a queue to perform BFS, processing nodes level by level.
options.C: Perform inorder traversal and use a stack to track levels.
options.D: Use a priority queue ordered by level.

questions.detailed_reasoning: BFS using a queue is the standard approach. Initialize a queue with the root. While the queue is not empty, process all nodes at the current level: pop nodes, add their values to the current level list, then push their children. Add the level list to the result. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST). A BST is valid if the left subtree contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key, and the right subtree contains only nodes with keys greater than the node's key.

options.A: Perform inorder traversal and check if the result is sorted.
options.B: Recursively check if node.val > max of left and node.val < min of right.
options.C: Perform DFS with min and max bounds for each node.
options.D: Perform BFS and compare each node with its parent.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The recursive approach with bounds is robust. At each node, ensure its value lies between a min and max bound. For the left child, update the max bound to the current node's value. For the right child, update the min bound to the current node's value. Inorder traversal (Option A) also works but uses extra space. [citation:8]

questions.title: Number of Islands
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n 2D binary grid which represents '1' (land) and '0' (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.

options.A: Iterate through the grid, when you find '1', perform BFS/DFS to mark all connected land as visited, and increment count.
options.B: Count the number of '1's that have no adjacent '1's above or left.
options.C: Use a union-find (disjoint set union) data structure.
options.D: Sort the grid by row and count clusters.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The classic approach is to iterate through each cell. When a '1' is found, increment the count and perform DFS or BFS to flood-fill the entire island (changing '1' to '0' or using a visited set). Union-Find (Option C) is also correct but more complex. O(m * n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Climbing Stairs
questions.problem_statement: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

options.A: This is the Fibonacci sequence: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2).
options.B: Use permutations: count the number of ways to arrange 1s and 2s.
options.C: Use a greedy algorithm: take as many 2s as possible.
options.D: Use BFS to explore all possible step combinations.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is a classic DP problem. To reach step n, you can come from step n-1 (taking 1 step) or step n-2 (taking 2 steps). The recurrence is dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2] with base cases dp[1] = 1, dp[2] = 2. This is exactly the Fibonacci sequence. [citation:3]

questions.title: House Robber
questions.problem_statement: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed. Adjacent houses have security systems, so you cannot rob two adjacent houses. Given an integer array nums representing the money in each house, return the maximum amount you can rob tonight.

options.A: Rob every alternate house (odd or even indices) and return the max.
options.B: Use recursion with memoization: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).
options.C: Sort the houses by value and rob the highest ones that are not adjacent.
options.D: Use a greedy algorithm to always rob the house with the highest value.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The DP solution works because at each house you have two choices: skip it (value = dp[i-1]) or rob it (value = dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Take the maximum. This can be done with O(1) space using two variables instead of an array. [citation:3]

questions.title: Coin Change
questions.problem_statement: You are given coins of different denominations and a total amount of money amount. Write a function to compute the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount. If that amount cannot be made up by any combination, return -1.

options.A: Use a greedy algorithm: always take the largest coin possible.
options.B: Use BFS on the state space (current amount).
options.C: Use DP: dp[i] = min(dp[i - coin] + 1) for all coins.
options.D: Use recursion to try all combinations of coins.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Dynamic Programming is the correct approach. Initialize dp[0] = 0 and dp[i] = INF for others. For each amount i, for each coin, if i - coin >= 0, dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1). Greedy (Option A) fails for denominations like [1, 3, 4] and amount 6. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived by deleting some elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.

options.A: Use 2D DP to compare all elements (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use patience sorting with binary search to achieve O(n log n).
options.C: Sort the array and find the longest common subsequence.
options.D: Use a sliding window to find increasing subarrays.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The O(n log n) solution is optimal for interviews (though O(n^2) DP is easier to explain). Maintain an array 'tails' where tails[i] is the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of length i+1. For each number, binary search to find the leftmost tails element that is >= num and replace it. [citation:3]

questions.title: Reverse Linked List
questions.problem_statement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the reversed list.

options.A: Use a stack to push all nodes, then pop and reconnect.
options.B: Use recursion: reverse the rest of the list and attach the current node at the end.
options.C: Use iterative method with three pointers (prev, current, next).
options.D: Copy the list values to an array, reverse the array, and rebuild the list.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The iterative three-pointer method is the most common solution: set prev = null, current = head. While current is not null, store current.next, set current.next = prev, move prev to current, and current to next. This reverses in-place in O(n) time and O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Linked List Cycle
questions.problem_statement: Given head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. A cycle occurs if some node in the list can be reached again by following the next pointer.

options.A: Use a hash set to store visited nodes.
options.B: Use Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare).
options.C: Store the length of the list and check if we exceed it while traversing.
options.D: Reverse the list and check if we end up at the head again.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare) uses O(1) space. Move slow pointer by 1 and fast pointer by 2. If they meet, a cycle exists. If fast reaches null, no cycle. Option A (hash set) also works but uses O(n) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
questions.problem_statement: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists. Merge the two lists into one sorted list and return the head of the merged list.

options.A: Append list2 to list1 and sort the entire list.
options.B: Use recursion: compare heads, set next to the result of merging the rest.
options.C: Use iterative two-pointer approach with a dummy head.
options.D: Store all values in an array, sort, and rebuild.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The iterative approach is efficient and avoids recursion stack overhead. Use a dummy head to simplify handling the start. Compare the current nodes of both lists and attach the smaller one to the result. Move the pointer of the list from which the node was taken. O(m + n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
questions.problem_statement: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

options.A: Traverse to find the length, then traverse again to remove the (L - n)th node.
options.B: Use two pointers with a gap of n nodes.
options.C: Use a stack to store nodes, then pop n nodes and reconnect.
options.D: Reverse the list, remove the nth node from the beginning, and reverse back.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pointer approach does it in one pass. Move a fast pointer n steps ahead, then move both slow and fast until fast reaches the end. The slow pointer will be just before the node to remove. Adjust pointers accordingly. Option A is also valid but requires two passes. [citation:3]

questions.title: Palindrome Linked List
questions.problem_statement: Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome, else false.

options.A: Copy values to an array and check palindrome using two pointers.
options.B: Find the middle, reverse the second half, and compare the two halves.
options.C: Use a stack to push half the nodes and compare when popping.
options.D: Use recursion to compare the first and last nodes.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The O(1) space approach: use slow and fast pointers to find the middle. Reverse the second half of the linked list. Then compare the first half with the reversed second half. If all match, it's a palindrome. Option A (array) is simpler but uses O(n) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
questions.problem_statement: Implement a first-in-first-out (FIFO) queue using only two stacks. The implemented queue should support push, pop, peek, and empty operations.

options.A: Use one stack for input and one for output. On pop, if output stack is empty, pop all from input to output.
options.B: Use two stacks but always push to stack1 and always pop from stack2.
options.C: Use a single stack and reverse it on every operation.
options.D: Use a stack for enqueue and a queue for dequeue.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is the classic 'two-stack queue'. For enqueue, push onto input stack. For dequeue, if output stack is empty, pop all elements from input stack and push them onto output stack (reversing order). Then pop from output stack. This gives amortized O(1) per operation. [citation:3]

questions.title: Implement Stack using Queues
questions.problem_statement: Implement a last-in-first-out (LIFO) stack using only two queues. The implemented stack should support push, pop, top, and empty operations.

options.A: Always push to queue1; on pop, move all except the last element from queue1 to queue2.
options.B: Use a single queue and rotate elements to bring the last element to the front.
options.C: Use two queues but maintain the stack property by always keeping one queue empty.
options.D: Use one queue for push and another for pop operations.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Using a single queue for stack implementation is efficient. For push, simply add to queue. For pop, rotate the queue (move front to back) size-1 times, then pop the front. This makes the last pushed element become the front. Option A also works but Option B is cleaner. [citation:8]

questions.title: Min Stack
questions.problem_statement: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time. All operations (push, pop, top, getMin) must run in O(1) time.

options.A: Maintain an additional stack that keeps track of the current minimum at each level.
options.B: Store pairs (value, current_min) in a single stack.
options.C: Maintain a variable min and update it on push and pop, but pop may require O(n) to find new min.
options.D: Use a priority queue alongside the stack.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The auxiliary stack approach: When pushing a value, if the auxiliary stack is empty or the value is <= the top of the auxiliary stack, push it onto the auxiliary stack. When popping, if the popped value equals the top of the auxiliary stack, pop from the auxiliary stack as well. getMin returns the top of the auxiliary stack. [citation:3]

questions.title: LRU Cache
questions.problem_statement: Design a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache that supports get(key) and put(key, value) operations in O(1) average time complexity. When the cache reaches its capacity, it should evict the least recently used item before inserting a new one.

options.A: Use a dictionary and maintain a timestamp for each entry, evict based on timestamp.
options.B: Use a combination of a hash map and a doubly linked list.
options.C: Use two stacks to maintain recency order.
options.D: Use an array and move elements when accessed.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard solution uses a hash map for O(1) lookup and a doubly linked list to maintain the order of usage. When an item is accessed, move it to the head (most recent). When capacity is exceeded, remove the tail (least recent). This gives O(1) time for both operations. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find the Missing Number
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.

options.A: Sort the array and find the index where nums[i] != i.
options.B: Use the sum formula: n*(n+1)/2 - sum(nums).
options.C: Use XOR: XOR all indices and all numbers, the result is the missing number.
options.D: Use a hash set to store all numbers and check for missing.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The XOR solution is elegant and avoids overflow issues that sum might have. XOR all numbers from 0 to n together, then XOR with all numbers in the array. Since a XOR a = 0, the remaining value is the missing number. Option B (sum) is also correct but can cause integer overflow in some languages. [citation:1]

questions.title: Move Zeroes
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, move all 0's to the end of it while maintaining the relative order of the non-zero elements. You must do this in-place without making a copy of the array.

options.A: Count zeros, create a new array of non-zero elements, and append zeros.
options.B: Use two pointers: one for iterating, one for placing non-zero elements.
options.C: Sort the array, zeros will automatically go to the end.
options.D: Swap each zero with the next non-zero element repeatedly.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The optimal approach uses a 'slow' pointer that tracks where the next non-zero element should go. Iterate with a 'fast' pointer. When a non-zero is encountered, swap it with the element at slow, then increment slow. This preserves order and is O(n) time with O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums with n objects colored red (0), white (1), or blue (2), sort them in-place so that objects of the same color are adjacent, in the order red, white, and blue. You must solve this without using a sorting library.

options.A: Count the number of 0s, 1s, and 2s, then overwrite the array.
options.B: Use three pointers: low, mid, high. Move 0s to the front, 2s to the back.
options.C: Use bubble sort to bubble 2s to the end and 0s to the front.
options.D: Use quicksort partition with 1 as the pivot.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The Dutch National Flag algorithm (one-pass O(n) with O(1) space) uses three pointers. low tracks the boundary of 0s, mid scans the array, high tracks the boundary of 2s. When nums[mid] == 0, swap with low, increment low and mid. When nums[mid] == 2, swap with high, decrement high. When nums[mid] == 1, increment mid. [citation:1][citation:3]

questions.title: Intersection of Two Arrays II
questions.problem_statement: Given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, return an array of their intersection. Each element in the result must appear as many times as it shows in both arrays, and you may return the result in any order.

options.A: Sort both arrays and use two pointers to find common elements.
options.B: Use a hash map to count frequencies from the smaller array, then iterate through the larger array.
options.C: Use a hash set to store unique elements from the first array, then check the second.
options.D: Nested loops to check each element (O(n*m)).

questions.detailed_reasoning: HashMap gives O(m + n) time and O(min(m, n)) space. Count frequencies of the smaller array in a hash map. Then iterate through the larger array; if an element exists in the map with count > 0, add it to the result and decrement the count. Sorting (Option A) also works but modifies the original arrays. [citation:1]

questions.title: First Unique Character in a String
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, find the first non-repeating character in it and return its index. If it does not exist, return -1.

options.A: For each character, scan the rest of the string to see if it repeats.
options.B: Use two passes: first pass to count frequencies, second pass to find the first character with frequency 1.
options.C: Use a queue to track characters in order of appearance.
options.D: Sort the string and find the first unique character.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Two-pass with hash map: first pass counts frequency of each character. Second pass iterates through the string and returns the index of the first character whose frequency is 1. This is O(n) time and O(1) space (since only 26 letters if lowercase). [citation:3]

questions.title: Valid Anagram
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise. An anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase.

options.A: Sort both strings and compare.
options.B: Use a frequency array of size 26 to count characters.
options.C: Use a hash map to store counts from s and decrement from t.
options.D: All of the above are valid approaches.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All three approaches are valid. Sorting is O(n log n). Frequency array is O(n) with O(1) space (if only lowercase letters). Hash map works for Unicode characters as well. Any of these will pass the test cases. [citation:1][citation:3]

questions.title: Valid Palindrome
questions.problem_statement: A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters to lowercase and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome, otherwise false.

options.A: Reverse the cleaned string and compare.
options.B: Use two pointers: one from the start, one from the end, skipping non-alphanumeric characters.
options.C: Use a stack to push characters and pop them for comparison.
options.D: Use recursion to compare the first and last characters.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pointer approach is most efficient (O(n) time, O(1) space). Move both pointers inward, skipping non-alphanumeric characters, and compare characters after converting to lowercase. If at any point they don't match, return false. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums of n integers where nums[i] is in the range [1, n], return an array of all the integers in the range [1, n] that do not appear in nums. Solve it without extra space and in O(n) time.

options.A: Use a hash set to mark seen numbers and then iterate from 1 to n.
options.B: Sort the array and find gaps.
options.C: Mark visited indices by making the value at that index negative.
options.D: Use the sum formula to find missing numbers.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The O(1) space approach uses the array itself to mark visited numbers. For each number x, mark the element at index |x|-1 as negative. Then the indices with positive values are the missing numbers (index+1). This works because 1 <= nums[i] <= n. [citation:3]

questions.title: Majority Element
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums of size n, return the majority element (the element that appears more than ⌊n/2⌋ times). You may assume the majority element always exists.

options.A: Sort the array and return the middle element.
options.B: Use a hash map to count frequencies.
options.C: Use Boyer-Moore Majority Vote Algorithm (O(n) time, O(1) space).
options.D: All of the above are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All three options are valid. Sorting gives the median (O(n log n)). HashMap gives O(n) time and O(n) space. Boyer-Moore is optimal with O(n) time and O(1) space, using a candidate and count approach. [citation:3]

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
questions.problem_statement: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the i-th day. You may complete as many transactions as you like (buy and sell multiple times). Return the maximum profit you can achieve.

options.A: Buy at local minima and sell at local maxima.
options.B: Add up all positive differences between consecutive days.
options.C: Use dynamic programming to track holding and not holding states.
options.D: All of the above are valid strategies.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B, the 'peak-valley' approach, is the simplest: sum up price[i] - price[i-1] whenever price[i] > price[i-1]. This works because you can consider each positive increment as a separate transaction. Option A requires finding local minima and maxima, which is similar. DP also works. [citation:8]

questions.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, return the total number of continuous subarrays whose sum equals to k.

options.A: Generate all subarrays (O(n^2)) and check their sums.
options.B: Use prefix sums with a hash map to store frequencies of prefix sums.
options.C: Use a sliding window (only works for positive numbers).
options.D: Use binary search on prefix sums.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The hash map technique is optimal: maintain a running sum and a map that stores the frequency of prefix sums encountered. For each element, add it to sum. The number of subarrays ending at current index with sum k is map[sum - k]. Update the map with the current sum. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Merge Intervals
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i], merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals.

options.A: Sort intervals by start time, then merge overlapping intervals in one pass.
options.B: Use a stack to push intervals and merge with the top when overlapping.
options.C: Use a graph where overlaps are edges and find connected components.
options.D: Sort by end time and merge from the end.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sort by start time (O(n log n)). Initialize the merged list with the first interval. For each subsequent interval, if it overlaps with the last merged interval (current_start <= last_end), merge by updating last_end = max(last_end, current_end). Otherwise, add as a new interval. [citation:3]

questions.title: Next Permutation
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, find the next permutation in lexicographic order. If such arrangement is not possible, it must rearrange to the lowest possible order (sorted ascending). The transformation must be done in-place.

options.A: Generate all permutations (O(n!)) and find the next one.
options.B: Find the longest decreasing suffix, swap the pivot with the next larger element, and reverse the suffix.
options.C: Sort the array and use next_permutation from C++ STL.
options.D: Use a stack to find the next element.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The algorithm is: 1) Find the largest i such that nums[i] < nums[i+1] (the pivot). If none, reverse the whole array. 2) Find the largest j > i such that nums[j] > nums[i]. 3) Swap nums[i] and nums[j]. 4) Reverse the suffix from i+1 to the end. This gives O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Spiral Matrix
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n matrix, return all elements of the matrix in spiral order (starting from the top-left corner, moving right, down, left, up, and repeating).

options.A: Use a queue to process layer by layer.
options.B: Maintain four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right. Traverse in four directions and shrink boundaries.
options.C: Transpose the matrix and then traverse rows.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The boundary method is standard. While top <= bottom and left <= right: traverse left to right along top row (top++), traverse top to bottom along right column (right--), traverse right to left along bottom row (bottom--), traverse bottom to top along left column (left++). O(m * n) time. [citation:8]

questions.title: Rotate Image (Matrix)
questions.problem_statement: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image. Rotate the image by 90 degrees clockwise in-place.

options.A: Transpose the matrix, then reverse each row.
options.B: Rotate layer by layer (four-way swap).
options.C: Use an extra matrix to copy rotated values.
options.D: Both A and B are valid in-place approaches.

questions.detailed_reasoning: For a 90-degree clockwise rotation, Option A: transpose (swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]) then reverse each row. Option B: process layers and perform a four-way swap (top->right, right->bottom, bottom->left, left->top). Both are O(n^2) time with O(1) extra space. [citation:8]

questions.title: Set Matrix Zeroes
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n integer matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0's. You must do it in-place using constant extra space.

options.A: Use two arrays to mark which rows and columns need to be zeroed.
options.B: Use the first row and first column as the marker arrays.
options.C: Use a hash set to store rows and columns to zero.
options.D: After finding zeros, iterate again and set row and column to zero.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The O(1) space approach: use the first row to mark which columns should be zeroed, and the first column to mark which rows should be zeroed. Use two additional variables to store whether the first row/column themselves should be zeroed. Then traverse and zero accordingly. [citation:8]

questions.title: Word Search
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n grid of characters and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically). The same letter may not be used more than once.

options.A: Use BFS starting from each cell.
options.B: Use DFS with backtracking and a visited set to avoid reuse.
options.C: Use dynamic programming to store matching substrings.
options.D: Use a trie to store all possible words from the grid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DFS with backtracking is the standard approach: for each cell, if its character matches the first character of the word, start DFS. On each step, mark the cell as visited (or modify it), explore all 4 directions, then backtrack (restore the cell). O(m * n * 4^L) where L is word length. [citation:3]

questions.title: Generate Parentheses
questions.problem_statement: Given n pairs of parentheses, write a function to generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.

options.A: Use recursion with two counters: open and close. Add '(' if open < n, add ')' if close < open.
options.B: Use a stack to track opening parentheses and generate all combinations.
options.C: Use dynamic programming where dp[i] = '(' + dp[j] + ')' + dp[i-j-1].

questions.detailed_reasoning: The backtracking approach is intuitive. Maintain open and close counts. If open < n, add '(' and recurse. If close < open, add ')' and recurse. When open == close == n, add the string to the result. The number of combinations is the Catalan number. [citation:3]

questions.title: Permutations
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all possible permutations of the array.

options.A: Use the built-in next_permutation repeatedly.
options.B: Use backtracking with a used array to track selected elements.
options.C: Use a swap-based approach: swap element into position, recurse, then swap back.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All three approaches work. The swap-based backtracking (Option C) is popular for in-place generation. The used array approach (Option B) is intuitive. Option A works by starting from the sorted array and calling next_permutation until the largest permutation. All have O(n * n!) time. [citation:8]

questions.title: Subsets
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets.

options.A: Use bitmask: iterate from 0 to 2^n - 1 and include elements where bit is set.
options.B: Use backtracking: at each index, either include the element or skip it.
options.C: Use iterative: start with empty set and add each number to existing subsets.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All three approaches work. Bitmask is efficient for n <= 20. Backtracking is standard. The iterative method is also valid: start with [[]], for each num, extend each subset by adding num and append to result. All are O(n * 2^n). [citation:8]

questions.title: Combination Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of distinct integers candidates and a target integer target, return a list of all unique combinations where the chosen numbers sum to target. The same number may be chosen an unlimited number of times.

options.A: Use recursion: at each index, either take the number (stay at same index) or move to the next index.
options.B: Use dynamic programming where dp[i] stores all combinations summing to i.
options.C: Use BFS to explore all sums.
options.D: Use a greedy algorithm: pick the largest number first.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard backtracking solution for unbounded knapsack: at each step, you have the option to include the current element (and stay at the same index for the next recursive call) or skip it (move to the next index). This ensures that order doesn't matter and duplicates are avoided. [citation:3]

questions.title: Palindrome Partitioning
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, partition s such that every substring of the partition is a palindrome. Return all possible palindrome partitioning of s.

options.A: Use backtracking: at each index, check all substrings starting at index; if palindrome, add and recurse.
options.B: Precompute palindrome table using DP, then use backtracking.
options.C: Use BFS to build partitions level by level.
options.D: Use a stack to push and pop partitions.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard approach: DFS/backtracking with a start index. For each end index from start to n-1, check if s[start:end+1] is a palindrome. If yes, add it to the current partition and recurse from end+1. Palindrome checking can be optimized with a precomputed DP table. [citation:8]

questions.title: Single Number
questions.problem_statement: Given a non-empty array of integers nums, every element appears twice except for one. Find that single one. Your algorithm should have linear runtime and use only constant extra space.

options.A: Sort the array and look for the element without a pair.
options.B: Use a hash set to track seen numbers.
options.C: Use XOR: XOR all numbers together. The result is the single number.
options.D: Use sum of the array minus 2 * sum of unique elements.

questions.detailed_reasoning: XOR is optimal: a XOR a = 0, and XOR is commutative and associative. XORing all numbers together will cancel out the pairs, leaving only the single number. This is O(n) time and O(1) space. Option B works but uses O(n) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Number of 1 Bits (Hamming Weight)
questions.problem_statement: Write a function that takes the binary representation of a positive integer and returns the number of '1' bits it has (also known as the Hamming weight).

options.A: Convert to string and count '1's.
options.B: Use n & (n-1) to clear the lowest set bit in each iteration.
options.C: Use right shift and check n & 1.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All approaches work. Option B (n & (n-1)) is optimal because it only loops as many times as there are 1 bits (instead of all 32 bits). Option C loops 32 times. Option A uses string conversion, which is less efficient but works. [citation:3]

questions.title: Reverse Bits
questions.problem_statement: Reverse the bits of a given 32-bit unsigned integer and return the resulting number.

options.A: Convert to string, reverse, and convert back.
options.B: Use a loop to extract bits and build the result (res = (res << 1) | (n & 1)).
options.C: Use a lookup table to reverse 8 bits at a time.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B is the standard interview solution: for i from 0 to 31, result = (result << 1) | (n & 1); n >>= 1. Option C (lookup table) is faster for multiple calls. Option A works but is less efficient. All are acceptable. [citation:3]

questions.title: Missing Number (XOR approach)
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.

options.A: Sort the array and find where nums[i] != i.
options.B: Use sum formula: n*(n+1)/2 - sum(nums).
options.C: XOR all numbers from 0 to n, then XOR with all elements. The result is the missing number.
options.D: Use a hash set to store all numbers and check for missing.

questions.detailed_reasoning: XOR solution is elegant: missing = 0; for i from 0 to n: missing ^= i; for each num in nums: missing ^= num. Since a ^ a = 0, pairs cancel, leaving the missing number. It avoids overflow issues that sum might have. [citation:3]

questions.title: Binary Search
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search for target in nums. If target exists, return its index, otherwise return -1.

options.A: Iterate through the array (O(n)).
options.B: Use binary search: set left=0, right=len-1; while left <= right, check mid.
options.C: Use recursion to divide the array into halves.
options.D: Use a hash map to map values to indices for O(1) lookup.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search gives O(log n) time. The iterative approach is preferred to avoid recursion stack overhead. At each step, calculate mid = left + (right - left) / 2 to avoid overflow. If nums[mid] == target, return mid; if less, search right; if greater, search left. [citation:3]

questions.title: First Bad Version
questions.problem_statement: You are given an API bool isBadVersion(version) which returns whether a version is bad. You need to find the first bad version among versions 1 through n, minimizing the number of API calls.

options.A: Check sequentially from version 1 to n.
options.B: Use binary search to find the first true in a boolean array.
options.C: Use exponential search, then binary search.
options.D: Use a queue to check versions in batches.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search is optimal. The pattern is: find the first index where isBadVersion(mid) is true. While left < right, mid = left + (right - left)/2. If isBadVersion(mid) is false, search right (left = mid+1). If true, search left (right = mid). This returns the first bad version in O(log n) calls. [citation:3]

questions.title: Search Insert Position
questions.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of distinct integers and a target value, return the index if the target is found. If not, return the index where it would be inserted to maintain order. Your algorithm must run in O(log n) time.

options.A: Linear scan to find the correct position.
options.B: Binary search: if target found, return index; else return left after the loop.
options.C: Use bisect_left from Python's bisect module.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search works perfectly. After the standard binary search loop (while left <= right), if target is not found, left will be the insertion point. So return left. Option C is correct if we can use libraries. [citation:3]

questions.title: Sqrt(x)
questions.problem_statement: Given a non-negative integer x, return the square root of x rounded down to the nearest integer. The returned integer should be the greatest integer less than or equal to the square root of x.

options.A: Use Python's math.isqrt(x).
options.B: Use binary search: find the largest mid such that mid*mid <= x.
options.C: Use Newton's method (iterative approximation).

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search is the standard interview solution: search between 1 and x (or x/2+1 for efficiency). While left <= right, if mid*mid == x, return mid; if mid*mid < x, move left up; if greater, move right down. Return right after the loop. Newton's method converges faster but is more complex. [citation:3]

questions.title: Peak Index in a Mountain Array
questions.problem_statement: Given a mountain array arr where arr[0] < arr[1] < ... < arr[i] > arr[i+1] > ... > arr[n-1], return the peak index i. This is guaranteed to exist.

options.A: Linear scan to find where arr[i] > arr[i+1].
options.B: Binary search: if arr[mid] < arr[mid+1], peak is on the right; else on the left.
options.C: Use two pointers from both ends.
options.D: Use recursion to find the maximum element.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search gives O(log n). Since the array has a single peak, we can compare arr[mid] with arr[mid+1]. If arr[mid] < arr[mid+1], the peak is to the right (left = mid+1). Otherwise, the peak is to the left or at mid (right = mid). The peak is at left when the loop ends. [citation:3]

questions.title: Koko Eating Bananas
questions.problem_statement: Koko loves to eat bananas. There are n piles of bananas with piles[i] bananas in the i-th pile. The guard will come back in h hours. Koko can decide her eating speed k (bananas per hour). Return the minimum integer k such that she can eat all bananas within h hours.

options.A: Try all possible speeds from 1 to max(piles).
options.B: Binary search on the answer: search for the minimum k where total hours <= h.
options.C: Use a greedy algorithm to eat from the largest pile first.
options.D: Use a priority queue to always eat from the largest pile.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is a classic 'find minimum valid value' binary search problem. The search space is from 1 to max(piles). For a candidate k, compute the total hours needed: sum(ceil(pile/k)). If total_hours <= h, we can try a smaller k; else, we need a larger k. O(n log(max(piles))). [citation:3]

questions.title: Inorder Traversal of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the inorder traversal of its nodes' values. (Left -> Root -> Right).

options.A: Use recursion: inorder(left), visit root, inorder(right).
options.B: Use iterative approach with a stack.
options.C: Use Morris Traversal for O(1) space.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Recursion is simplest but uses O(h) stack space. Iterative stack simulation also uses O(h) space. Morris Traversal uses O(1) extra space by temporarily modifying the tree (threading). For interviews, recursion is usually acceptable. [citation:8]

questions.title: Preorder Traversal
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the preorder traversal of its nodes' values (Root -> Left -> Right).

options.A: Recursion: visit root, preorder(left), preorder(right).
options.B: Iterative: use a stack, push right first then left.
options.C: Use Morris traversal by modifying the tree.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All approaches work. For iterative stack, we push the right child first then left child, so that left is processed first (LIFO). Morris traversal for preorder is slightly different from inorder but also works in O(1) space. [citation:8]

questions.title: Postorder Traversal
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the postorder traversal of its nodes' values (Left -> Right -> Root).

options.A: Recursion: postorder(left), postorder(right), visit root.
options.B: Iterative: use two stacks, or one stack with a visited flag.
options.C: Use Morris traversal (more complex).

questions.detailed_reasoning: A neat trick: preorder is Root-Left-Right. If we modify preorder to Root-Right-Left, then reversing that gives Left-Right-Root, which is postorder. This can be implemented iteratively easily. Recursive is simple too. [citation:8]

questions.title: Symmetric Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, check whether it is a mirror of itself (i.e., symmetric around its center).

options.A: Check if the left and right subtrees are mirrors of each other using recursion.
options.B: Use level order traversal and check if each level is a palindrome.
options.C: Use two queues for BFS, one from left and one from right.
options.D: All of the above are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The recursive approach: a tree is symmetric if the left subtree is a mirror of the right subtree. Two trees are mirrors if their roots have the same value and the left of one is mirror of the right of the other. BFS (Option C) is also valid. [citation:8]

questions.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the diameter. The diameter is the length of the longest path between any two nodes. This path may or may not pass through the root.

options.A: For each node, calculate left height + right height and track the maximum.
options.B: Use BFS to find the farthest node from an arbitrary node, then find the farthest from that node.
options.C: Use recursion to return the height while updating a global diameter value.
options.D: Both A and C are standard.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The optimal O(n) solution: perform DFS, and for each node, compute the height of its left and right subtrees. The candidate diameter at that node is left_height + right_height. Update a global maximum. Return 1 + max(left_height, right_height) as the height to the parent. [citation:3]

questions.title: Balanced Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given a binary tree, determine if it is height-balanced. A height-balanced binary tree is defined as a tree where the left and right subtrees of every node differ in height by no more than 1.

options.A: Calculate the height of each node, then check if the difference is <= 1.
options.B: Use DFS to compute heights; if any node is imbalanced, propagate -1 up.
options.C: Use BFS to check levels.
options.D: Use a stack and compute heights bottom-up.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B is the optimal O(n) approach: in the DFS function, compute the height of the subtree; if any subtree is unbalanced, return -1. Check if the difference between left height and right height > 1. This saves recomputation. Option A would be O(n log n) if done naively. [citation:8]

questions.title: Path Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree and an integer targetSum, return true if there is a root-to-leaf path such that the sum of the values along the path equals targetSum. A leaf is a node with no children.

options.A: Use recursion: if root is null, return false; if leaf, check if root.val == targetSum; else recurse on children with targetSum - root.val.
options.B: Use BFS with a queue storing (node, current_sum).
options.C: Use DFS with a stack.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All approaches work. The recursive DFS is the simplest: at each node, subtract the node's value from the target sum. If it reaches a leaf and the remaining sum is 0, return true. This is O(n) time. [citation:8]

questions.title: Binary Tree Paths
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return all root-to-leaf paths in any order.

options.A: Use DFS with backtracking to build the path string.
options.B: Use BFS with a queue storing (node, path_so_far).
options.C: Use recursion, passing down the current path.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DFS recursion is the most common. At each node, append its value to the current path. If it's a leaf, add the path to the result list. Otherwise, recurse on children with path + '->'. This runs in O(n) time. [citation:8]

questions.title: Same Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, write a function to check if they are the same or not (structurally identical and have the same node values).

options.A: Recursively compare: if both null, true; if one null, false; if values differ, false; else recurse on left and right.
options.B: Use level order traversal with two queues.
options.C: Use preorder traversal and compare results.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The recursive approach is simplest and most efficient O(n). BFS also works by comparing nodes level by level. Preorder traversal results comparison works if we also include null markers. [citation:8]

questions.title: Subtree of Another Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot, return true if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and node values as subRoot, and false otherwise.

options.A: Check if the trees are identical starting from root; if not, recurse on left and right children.
options.B: Serialize both trees and check if subRoot's serialization is a substring of root's serialization.
options.C: Use a hash of each subtree and compare.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A is the standard DFS approach: a function isSame(p, q) checks if two trees are identical. Then isSubtree(root, subRoot) returns true if isSame(root, subRoot) or isSubtree(root.left) or isSubtree(root.right). This is O(n * m) worst case. Serialization with unique markers is O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary search tree, and an integer k, return the k-th smallest value (1-indexed) in the BST.

options.A: Perform inorder traversal (which gives sorted order) and return the k-th element.
options.B: Use a stack to perform iterative inorder traversal and stop at the k-th element.
options.C: Augment each node with the size of its left subtree to find in O(h) time.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Inorder traversal gives O(n) time and O(h) space. Iterative with stack allows early termination (O(k)). If the BST is augmented with subtree sizes, we can achieve O(h) by comparing k with left size. For interviews, iterative inorder is sufficient. [citation:3]

questions.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the values of the nodes you can see when standing on the right side, ordered from top to bottom.

options.A: Perform BFS level order traversal and add the last node of each level.
options.B: Perform DFS with a depth parameter, always visit right child first.
options.C: Use a queue to process nodes level by level.
options.D: Both A and C are the same approach.

questions.detailed_reasoning: BFS is straightforward: for each level, the last node in the queue (or the node processed last) is added to the result. DFS (right-first) is also common: maintain a list and a depth, when entering a new depth, add the node to the result (since right nodes are visited first). [citation:3]

questions.title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder
questions.problem_statement: Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder where preorder is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and inorder is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.

options.A: Use a hash map to store inorder indices for O(1) lookup, then recursively build.
options.B: The first element in preorder is the root. Find its index in inorder; left part is left subtree, right part is right subtree.
options.C: Use a stack to simulate the construction.
options.D: Both A and B describe the standard solution.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard O(n) solution: map inorder values to their indices. In the recursive function, the first element of the preorder list is the root. Find its index in inorder. The elements to the left form the left subtree, and to the right form the right subtree. Recursively build. [citation:8]

questions.title: Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
questions.problem_statement: You are given a perfect binary tree where all leaves are at the same level and every parent has two children. Populate each next pointer to point to its next right node. If there is no next right node, set it to null.

options.A: Use BFS level order traversal and connect nodes at each level.
options.B: Use recursion: connect left child to right child, and right child to parent's next's left child.
options.C: Use an iterative approach that traverses level by level without a queue.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option C is the O(1) space solution: start at the leftmost node of the current level, connect children, then move to the next level. Option A uses O(n) space for the queue but is simpler. Recursive (Option B) works if we use the next pointers to connect cousins. [citation:3]

questions.title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, flatten the tree into a 'linked list' where the right child points to the next node in preorder traversal, and the left child is always null. The flattening must be done in-place.

options.A: Perform preorder traversal and store nodes, then rebuild.
options.B: Use recursion: flatten left, flatten right, then attach the flattened left to the right, and the flattened right to the end of that.
options.C: Use Morris-like traversal (O(1) space) by finding the rightmost node of the left subtree.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B (recursive) is standard: flatten left and right subtrees, then set node.right = flattened_left, and find the end of that to attach flattened_right. Option C (Morris) is more advanced but offers O(1) extra space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Number of Islands (Union-Find)
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n 2D binary grid which represents '1' (land) and '0' (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.

options.A: Perform DFS/BFS on each '1' cell.
options.B: Use Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) to union adjacent '1's.
options.C: Count '1's that have no '1' above or to the left in a scanned grid.
options.D: Both A and B are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DFS/BFS is simpler and runs in O(m * n). Union-Find also works: initially, each '1' is its own island. When encountering an adjacent '1', union them. At the end, count the number of unique roots that are land. Union-Find can be useful if we need dynamic connectivity. [citation:3]

questions.title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection in Graph)
questions.problem_statement: There are n courses labeled from 0 to n-1. You are given prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [a_i, b_i] indicates that you must take course b_i before course a_i. Return true if you can finish all courses, otherwise false.

options.A: Check if the graph has a cycle. If there is a cycle, it's impossible, else it's possible.
options.B: Use Kahn's algorithm (topological sort with indegrees).
options.C: Use DFS with three states (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect cycles.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is a cycle detection problem in a directed graph. Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based topological sort) uses indegrees: if the number of processed nodes < n, a cycle exists. DFS with states (0=unvisited, 1=visiting, 2=visited) also detects cycles when we encounter a visiting node. [citation:3]

questions.title: Clone Graph
questions.problem_statement: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy (clone) of the graph. Each node contains a value and a list of its neighbors.

options.A: Perform BFS and use a hash map to map original nodes to cloned nodes.
options.B: Perform DFS (recursion) with a hash map to avoid re-cloning.
options.C: Use a queue to process nodes and clone neighbors.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All approaches involve a hash map that stores the mapping from original node to cloned node. Then, traverse the graph using BFS or DFS; for each neighbor, if it hasn't been cloned yet, clone it; then add the neighbor's clone to the current node's neighbor list. [citation:3]

questions.title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n matrix heights where heights[r][c] is the height of the cell, water can flow to adjacent cells with equal or lower height. Water can flow to the Pacific Ocean from the top and left edges, and to the Atlantic Ocean from the bottom and right edges. Return a list of cells where water can flow to both oceans.

options.A: Perform DFS/BFS from all Pacific border cells and mark reachable cells; do the same for Atlantic. Return intersection.
options.B: Perform DFS from each cell and check if it can reach both oceans (O(m^2 * n^2)).
options.C: Use dynamic programming to store reachability.
options.D: Use union-find to connect cells to oceans.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A is optimal: perform BFS/DFS starting from all Pacific border cells (top and left) to find all cells that can flow to the Pacific. Similarly from Atlantic border cells (bottom and right). A cell that appears in both sets is the answer. O(m * n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Surrounded Regions
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n matrix board containing 'X' and 'O', capture all regions that are surrounded by 'X'. A region is captured if it is not connected to the border of the board. Any 'O' that is on the border or connected to the border should remain unchanged.

options.A: Mark all border-connected 'O's using BFS/DFS, then flip all unmarked 'O's to 'X'.
options.B: Flip all 'O's to 'X' and then restore border-connected ones.
options.C: Use union-find to connect 'O's to a dummy border node.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Standard approach: iterate over border cells. For each 'O' on the border, perform DFS/BFS to mark all connected 'O's as safe (e.g., change to 'T'). Then iterate through the board: change all 'O's (unsafe) to 'X', and change all 'T's (safe) back to 'O'. O(m * n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Word Ladder
questions.problem_statement: Given two words beginWord and endWord, and a dictionary wordList, return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord, such that only one letter can be changed at a time and each transformed word must exist in the wordList. Return 0 if no such sequence exists.

options.A: Perform BFS on the state graph where nodes are words, edges connect words differing by one letter.
options.B: Use bidirectional BFS to reduce the search space.
options.C: Generate all possible wildcard patterns for each word (e.g., 'h*t') and use a map.
options.D: All of the above are valid optimization strategies.

questions.detailed_reasoning: BFS is the standard approach. For efficiency, generate all possible intermediate patterns (e.g., word = 'hot' -> '*ot', 'h*t', 'ho*') and use a map from pattern to list of words. Bidirectional BFS from beginWord and endWord gives better speed. [citation:3]

questions.title: Topological Sort (Kahn's Algorithm)
questions.problem_statement: Given a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with n nodes and a list of edges, return a topological ordering of the nodes. If the graph has a cycle, return an empty list.

options.A: Use DFS: after processing neighbors, add node to stack; reverse the stack.
options.B: Use Kahn's algorithm: repeatedly remove nodes with indegree 0.
options.C: Use BFS with indegree counts.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Both DFS and BFS (Kahn's) are standard. DFS approach: maintain a visited state (0=unvisited, 1=visiting, 2=visited). After processing all neighbors, push the node onto a stack. If we encounter a visiting node, a cycle exists. Kahn's algorithm uses indegrees and a queue. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find the Celebrity
questions.problem_statement: Suppose you are at a party with n people (labeled 0 to n-1), and there may be a celebrity. The celebrity is someone that everyone knows, but the celebrity knows no one. You are given an API knows(a, b) which returns true if a knows b. Find the celebrity in O(n) calls to the API.

options.A: Use a two-pass approach: first find a candidate, then verify.
options.B: Use elimination: if knows(i, j) is true, i cannot be celebrity; else j cannot be celebrity.
options.C: Use a graph to represent relationships and find a node with indegree n-1 and outdegree 0.
options.D: Both A and B describe the same optimal O(n) approach.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The optimal O(n) algorithm: initialize candidate = 0. For i from 1 to n-1, if knows(candidate, i) is true, candidate cannot be celebrity, so set candidate = i. After this loop, verify candidate by checking that everyone knows candidate and candidate knows no one. This uses 2n-2 calls. [citation:3]

questions.title: Decode Ways
questions.problem_statement: A message containing letters A-Z can be encoded into numbers using mapping: 'A' -> '1', 'B' -> '2', ..., 'Z' -> '26'. Given a string s containing only digits, return the number of ways to decode it.

options.A: Use recursion with memoization: ways(i) = ways(i+1) + ways(i+2) if a valid two-digit number exists.
options.B: Use iterative DP: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] (if valid).
options.C: Treat '0' carefully, as it can only be part of '10' or '20'.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is a classic DP problem. dpi represents the number of ways to decode the substring starting at i. Transition: add dpi+1 if s[i] != '0'; add dpi+2 if s[i:i+2] is between '10' and '26'. The iterative approach is O(n) time and O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Unique Paths
questions.problem_statement: A robot is located at the top-left corner of an m x n grid. The robot can only move either down or right at any point in time. The robot is trying to reach the bottom-right corner. How many possible unique paths are there?

options.A: Use combinatorics: C(m+n-2, m-1).
options.B: Use DP: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].
options.C: Use recursion with memoization.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The number of paths is the number of ways to choose m-1 downs from (m+n-2) total moves, which is C(m+n-2, m-1). DP is also straightforward: initialize first row and column to 1, then fill in the grid. Both are O(m*n) or O(min(m,n)) space optimizable. [citation:3]

questions.title: Unique Paths II (with Obstacles)
questions.problem_statement: Same as Unique Paths but now the grid may have obstacles (marked as 1). A robot cannot move through an obstacle.

options.A: Use DP: if grid[i][j] == 1, dp[i][j] = 0; else dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].
options.B: Use BFS to count paths.
options.C: Use recursion with memoization and obstacle check.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DP is straightforward: if the cell is an obstacle, set dp[i][j]=0. Otherwise, it's the sum from above and left. The first row/column need special handling: if any obstacle appears, all subsequent cells in that row/column are unreachable (value 0). [citation:3]

questions.title: Minimum Path Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n grid filled with non-negative numbers, find a path from the top-left to the bottom-right that minimizes the sum of numbers along the path. You can only move right or down.

options.A: Use DP: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
options.B: Use Dijkstra's algorithm on a grid.
options.C: Use greedy: always go right or down to the smaller number.
options.D: Both A and B are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DP is optimal O(m*n). Initialize dp[0][0]=grid[0][0]; first row: dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] + grid[0][j]; first column: dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] + grid[i][0]; then fill the rest. Greedy (Option C) does not always find the optimal path because future costs matter. [citation:3]

questions.title: Jump Game
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of non-negative integers nums, you are initially positioned at the first index. Each element represents the maximum jump length at that position. Return true if you can reach the last index, or false otherwise.

options.A: Use BFS to explore reachable positions.
options.B: Use DP: dp[i] represents if we can reach i.
options.C: Use greedy: keep track of the farthest index reachable.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The greedy O(n) solution: maintain 'reach' as the farthest index we can reach. Iterate i from 0 to n-1. If i > reach, we can't proceed. Otherwise, update reach = max(reach, i + nums[i]). If reach >= n-1 at the end, return true. [citation:3]

questions.title: Jump Game II
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of non-negative integers nums, you are at the first index. Each element represents the maximum jump length at that position. Return the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index. It is guaranteed that you can reach the last index.

options.A: Use BFS to find the shortest path.
options.B: Use DP with O(n^2) time.
options.C: Use greedy: track current_end and farthest, increment jumps when reaching current_end.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The greedy solution is O(n): maintain 'current_end' (the farthest we can reach with the current number of jumps), 'farthest' (the farthest we can reach overall). For each index, update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). When i reaches current_end, we need to take a jump, set current_end = farthest, and increment jumps. [citation:3]

questions.title: Gas Station
questions.problem_statement: There are n gas stations along a circular route. You are given two arrays gas[i] (gas at station i) and cost[i] (cost to go to next station). Return the starting station index if you can travel around the circuit once, otherwise -1.

options.A: For each station, simulate the journey (O(n^2)).
options.B: If total gas < total cost, it's impossible. Otherwise, find the station where the running sum of gas - cost is minimum.
options.C: Use two pointers: start and end.
options.D: Use greedy: start at 0, accumulate deficit; if deficit > 0, start from next station and reset deficit.

questions.detailed_reasoning: O(n) greedy solution: if total_gas < total_cost, return -1. For the starting index, start from 0, keep a running sum of tank. If the running sum ever becomes negative, reset the start to i+1 and reset the sum to 0. This works because if we can't reach i from start, we also can't reach i from any point before start. [citation:3]

questions.title: Candy
questions.problem_statement: There are n children standing in a line. Each child is assigned a rating value. You want to give each child at least 1 candy. Children with a higher rating get more candies than their neighbors. Return the minimum number of candies you need to distribute.

options.A: Traverse from left to right, then right to left, taking maximums.
options.B: Use a priority queue to assign candies.
options.C: Use BFS to propagate requirements.
options.D: Use dynamic programming to find the longest decreasing path.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pass greedy approach works: first pass (left to right): if ratings[i] > ratings[i-1], candies[i] = candies[i-1] + 1, else 1. Second pass (right to left): if ratings[i] > ratings[i+1], candies[i] = max(candies[i], candies[i+1] + 1). Sum up candies. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Task Scheduler
