questions.problem_statement: Given a characters array tasks representing the tasks a CPU needs to do, each letter represents a different task. Tasks could be done in any order. Each task takes 1 unit of time. There is a cooldown of n units of time between two same tasks. Return the least number of units of time required to finish all tasks.

options.A: Sort tasks and schedule the most frequent task first.
options.B: Use a max heap to always pick the task with the highest remaining count, and a queue to track cooldown.
options.C: Use formula: max((max_freq - 1) * (n + 1) + count_of_max_freq, len(tasks)).

questions.detailed_reasoning: The formula (Option C) gives O(n) time with counting. Suppose the most frequent task appears max_freq times. The minimum time is (max_freq - 1) * (n + 1) + number_of_tasks_with_max_freq. But if other tasks fill the idle slots completely, the answer is simply len(tasks) (no idle time). [citation:3]

questions.title: Partition Labels
questions.problem_statement: You are given a string s. Partition the string into as many parts as possible so that each letter appears in at most one part. Return a list of integers representing the size of these parts.

options.A: For each character, store its last occurrence index. Use two pointers to track the end of the current partition.
options.B: Use a sliding window and expand until all characters in the window have their last occurrence within the window.
options.C: Use a stack to push characters and pop when a partition ends.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Greedy O(n) solution: first pass: record the last index of each character. Second pass: maintain 'start' and 'end'. For each i from 0 to n-1, update 'end' = max(end, last[s[i]]). When i == end, we have a partition; add (end - start + 1) to result, and set start = i+1. [citation:3]

questions.title: Valid Parenthesis String
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s containing only '(', ')', and '*', where '*' can be treated as '(', ')', or an empty string, check if the string is valid (all parentheses properly matched).

options.A: Use two counters: low and high, representing the range of possible open parentheses.
options.B: Use a stack to handle '(' and '*'.
options.C: Use dynamic programming to check all possibilities.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The greedy O(n) solution: maintain 'low' (minimum possible open count) and 'high' (maximum possible open count). For each char: if '(': low++, high++; if ')': low--, high--; if '*': low--, high++. If high < 0, invalid. At any point, low = max(low, 0). At the end, return low == 0. [citation:3]

questions.title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
questions.problem_statement: Given a 1-indexed array of integers that is already sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific target. Return the indices of the two numbers (1-indexed).

options.A: Use binary search for each element (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use two pointers: left = 0, right = n-1. Move pointers based on sum vs target.
options.C: Use a hash map to store complements.
options.D: Both B and the hash map approach work.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pointer technique is optimal for sorted arrays (O(n)). Since the array is sorted, if sum > target, move the right pointer left; if sum < target, move the left pointer right. This avoids the extra space of a hash map. [citation:3]

questions.title: 3Sum Closest
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums of length n and an integer target, find three integers in nums such that the sum is closest to target. Return the sum of the three integers.

options.A: Sort the array, fix one element, and use two pointers to find the closest pair sum.
options.B: Use three nested loops (O(n^3)).
options.C: Use a hash set to store pair sums.
options.D: Use binary search for the third element.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sort the array (O(n log n)). For each index i, set left = i+1, right = n-1. While left < right, calculate sum = nums[i] + nums[left] + nums[right]. Update the closest sum if needed. If sum < target, left++; else if sum > target, right--; else return target exactly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: Given a sorted array nums, remove the duplicates in-place such that each unique element appears only once. Return the number of unique elements.

options.A: Use a hash set to track seen elements.
options.B: Use two pointers: 'slow' for the position of the next unique element, 'fast' to scan.
options.C: Use Python's built-in set and sort.
options.D: Both B and a hash set approach are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pointer in-place approach is optimal: if the array is sorted, duplicates are adjacent. Set 'slow' = 0. For 'fast' from 1 to n-1, if nums[fast] != nums[slow], increment slow and copy nums[fast] to nums[slow]. Return slow+1 as the length. O(n) time, O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Remove Element
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums and a value val, remove all instances of val in-place and return the new length. The order of the elements may be changed.

options.A: Use a slow pointer to track the next position for non-val elements.
options.B: Use two pointers from both ends: when left points to val, swap with right and decrement right.
options.C: Count the number of val elements and shift the array.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A: iterate with two pointers; when an element is not val, assign to slow and increment slow. Option B (two-pointer from ends) works when order doesn't matter, which is acceptable per the problem. Both are O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Trapping Rain Water
questions.problem_statement: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.

options.A: For each bar, find the max height to the left and right; water += min(left_max, right_max) - height[i].
options.B: Use two pointers from both ends: keep left_max and right_max; move the pointer with the smaller max inward.
options.C: Use a stack to maintain decreasing heights.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B (two pointers) is the most efficient (O(n) time, O(1) space). At each step, the water trapped at the current pointer is determined by the smaller of left_max and right_max. Option A (prefix/suffix max) also works but uses O(n) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
questions.problem_statement: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. Your algorithm must run in O(n) time.

options.A: Sort the array and find the longest consecutive run (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use a hash set to store all numbers. For each number, check if it's the start of a sequence (num-1 not in set).
options.C: Use union-find to merge consecutive numbers.
options.D: Both B and C achieve O(n) time.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B is the standard O(n) solution. Insert all numbers into a hash set. Iterate through the array; for each num, if num-1 is not in the set (meaning it's the start of a sequence), then count how many consecutive numbers exist (num+1, num+2, ...) and update the maximum length. [citation:3]

questions.title: Top K Frequent Elements
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.

options.A: Count frequencies with a hash map, then sort by frequency (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use a min-heap of size k to keep the top k frequent elements.
options.C: Use bucket sort: frequency as index (O(n)).

questions.detailed_reasoning: Bucket sort is optimal (O(n)): create an array of lists indexed by frequency. After counting frequencies, for each number, place it in bucket[frequency]. Then, iterate from the highest frequency down to 1, collecting elements until we have k. [citation:3]

questions.title: Encode and Decode TinyURL
questions.problem_statement: Design a service for TinyURL. Implement the encode and decode methods. The encode method should convert a long URL into a short URL, and decode should convert the short URL back to the original URL.

options.A: Use a counter and a hash map: encode returns base62 of the counter.
options.B: Use a random string generator and handle collisions.
options.C: Use a hash of the URL (e.g., MD5) and truncate.
options.D: All of the above are valid approaches.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All are valid in different contexts. Counter-based is simple and guaranteed unique but predictable. Random-based uses more space but is harder to guess. Hashing might have collisions and needs collision resolution. For interviews, a counter with base62 encoding is often sufficient. [citation:3]

questions.title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree into a string and deserialize that string back into the original tree structure. The algorithm should work for any binary tree.

options.A: Use preorder traversal with null markers (e.g., 'X' for null) and commas as delimiters.
options.B: Use level order traversal (BFS) to store the tree in a string.
options.C: Use a pair (preorder, inorder) but requires unique values.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Preorder with null markers is the most common. For deserialization, use a queue or pointer to read the string recursively. BFS is also valid. The preorder+inorder approach works if values are unique, but it requires O(n) extra space for storage. [citation:3]

questions.title: Design HashMap
questions.problem_statement: Design a HashMap without using any built-in hash table libraries. Implement the methods put(key, value), get(key), and remove(key).

options.A: Use an array of size 10^6 (since key range is 0-10^6).
options.B: Use chaining: an array of linked lists with a hash function.
options.C: Use open addressing with linear probing.
options.D: All of the above are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: For interviews, chaining is the most common. Use an array of buckets (e.g., 10000), each bucket is a linked list of key-value pairs. Hash function: key % bucket_size. For collisions, traverse the list. Option A works if the key range is small and known. [citation:3]

questions.title: Daily Temperatures
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers temperatures representing daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the i-th day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day with a warmer temperature, keep answer[i] = 0.

options.A: Use nested loops (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use a monotonic decreasing stack storing indices.
options.C: Use a hash map to store next warmer index.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The monotonic stack approach is O(n). Iterate through temperatures. While the stack is not empty and the current temperature > temperatures[stack[-1]], pop the index and set answer[popped] = current_index - popped. Then push the current index onto the stack. The stack maintains decreasing temperatures. [citation:3]

questions.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram's bar heights where the width of each bar is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

options.A: For each bar, expand to left and right to find the max width (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use a monotonic increasing stack to find the left and right boundaries for each bar.
options.C: Use divide and conquer: find the minimum bar and split.
options.D: Both B and C are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The monotonic stack approach gives O(n). Use a stack to store indices of increasing heights. When a bar is lower than the bar at the top of the stack, we pop the top and calculate the area using that bar as the smallest height. The width is (current_index - stack[-1] - 1). This finds the left and right boundaries efficiently. [citation:3]

questions.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
questions.problem_statement: Design a trie (prefix tree) with insert, search, and startsWith methods.

options.A: Each node contains an array of 26 child nodes and a boolean isEnd.
options.B: Each node contains a hash map of characters to child nodes.
options.C: Use a hash map to store all prefixes.
options.D: Both A and B are valid implementations.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A (array of size 26) is efficient for lowercase letters but wastes space. Option B (hash map) is more flexible for Unicode and saves space when the alphabet is large. Both provide O(L) time for operations, where L is the length of the word. [citation:3]

questions.title: Add and Search Word (Data structure design)
questions.problem_statement: Design a data structure that supports adding new words and searching for words, where the search query can contain '.' which matches any single character.

options.A: Use a trie; in search, if a '.' is encountered, recursively search all children.
options.B: Use a hash map keyed by word length with sets of words, and check patterns with regex.
options.C: Use a hash set of all words and brute-force check patterns.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The trie with DFS for '.' is the standard solution. The '.' character requires exploring all child nodes at that level. This gives O(alphabet_size^L) in the worst case, but alphabet size is small (26), and patterns with many '.' are rare. [citation:3]

questions.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
questions.problem_statement: Design a data structure that supports adding new words and searching for words, where the search query can contain '.' which matches any single character.

options.A: Use a trie with DFS for '.'.
options.B: Use a hash map keyed by word length with lists of words; for search, check each word.
options.C: Use a bloom filter for existence.
options.D: Both A and B are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A (Trie) gives better time complexity for search. Option B is simpler and may be acceptable for smaller datasets. For the design problem, Trie is the expected answer, but the interviewer may accept Option B if explained properly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Word Search II
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n board of characters and a list of words, return all words on the board. Each word must be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (vertical/horizontal). The same cell cannot be used more than once in a word.

options.A: For each word, perform DFS on the board (O(num_words * m * n)).
options.B: Build a trie of all words, then perform DFS on the board, pruning when the prefix is not in the trie.
options.C: Use BFS for each starting cell.
options.D: Use a hash set of all words and check all paths.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The trie-based approach is optimal. Build a trie from the word list. Then, for each cell on the board, run DFS that checks if the current path matches a prefix in the trie. When a word is found (isEnd=true), add it to the result and optionally mark it as found to avoid duplicates. [citation:3]

questions.title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
questions.problem_statement: You are given an integer array cost where cost[i] is the cost of the i-th step on a staircase. Once you pay the cost, you can either climb one or two steps. You can start from either index 0 or index 1. Return the minimum cost to reach the top of the floor (beyond the last index).

options.A: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]), with base dp[0]=cost[0], dp[1]=cost[1].
options.B: The top is after the last index, so the answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]).
options.C: Use O(1) space by storing only the last two values.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is a variation of climbing stairs with costs. The recurrence: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). At the end, the top is one step beyond the array, so the answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) because you can take one step from n-1 or two steps from n-2. [citation:3]

questions.title: House Robber II (Circular)
questions.problem_statement: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. All houses are arranged in a circle. The first house is the neighbor of the last house. Adjacent houses cannot be robbed. Given an integer array nums representing the money in each house, return the maximum amount you can rob.

options.A: Solve two cases: rob houses [0..n-2] and [1..n-1], take the maximum.
options.B: Use the same DP as House Robber I but handle the circular constraint.
options.C: Use a DP array with state representing whether the first house was robbed.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Since the houses are in a circle, you cannot rob both the first and last house. Therefore, split the problem into two linear subproblems: one excluding the last house (0 to n-2), and one excluding the first house (1 to n-1). Take the maximum of the two results. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Common Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from the string by deleting some characters without changing the order.

options.A: Use 2D DP: if text1[i]==text2[j]: dp[i][j]=1+dp[i-1][j-1]; else dp[i][j]=max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
options.B: Use recursion with memoization.
options.C: Use space-optimized DP: two rows of size n.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard DP recurrence: dp[i][j] = length of LCS of text1[:i] and text2[:j]. If chars match, it's 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise, take the max of dp[i-1][j] and dp[i][j-1]. Space can be optimized to O(min(m, n)) because only the previous row is needed. [citation:3]

questions.title: Edit Distance (Levenshtein Distance)
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. Allowed operations: insert, delete, replace.

options.A: Use 2D DP: if chars match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).
options.B: Use BFS on state space (i, j).
options.C: Use recursion with memoization.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DP is standard. dp[i][j] = edit distance between first i chars of word1 and first j chars of word2. If chars match, no extra operation. If not, choose the minimum among: delete (dp[i-1][j]), insert (dp[i][j-1]), or replace (dp[i-1][j-1]), then add 1. O(m*n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Coin Change II (Number of Combinations)
questions.problem_statement: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount. Return the number of combinations that make up that amount. You may assume you have infinite supply of each coin.

options.A: dp[i] = sum(dp[i - coin]), but this counts permutations.
options.B: Use 2D DP where dp[i][j] is the number of ways using first i coins to make amount j.
options.C: Use 1D DP with outer loop over coins to avoid counting permutations.
options.D: Both B and C are correct.

questions.detailed_reasoning: To count combinations (order doesn't matter), iterate over coins first: for each coin, for amount from coin to target, dp[amount] += dp[amount - coin]. This ensures each coin is considered only once in the order, preventing permutation duplicates. [citation:3]

questions.title: Target Sum
questions.problem_statement: You are given an integer array nums and an integer target. You can assign either '+' or '-' to each integer. Find the number of ways to assign symbols to make the sum of the integers equal to target.

options.A: This reduces to subset sum: find the number of subsets that sum to (sum + target)/2.
options.B: Use DP with offset to handle negative indices.
options.C: Use recursion with memoization (DFS with index and current sum).

questions.detailed_reasoning: Let P be the sum of numbers with '+' and N with '-'. Then P + N = total_sum, P - N = target. Solving gives P = (total_sum + target)/2. So the problem reduces to counting subsets summing to P. DP works: dp[i] = number of ways to achieve sum i. [citation:3]

questions.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return true if you can partition the array into two subsets with equal sum. If not, return false.

options.A: If total_sum is odd, return false. Otherwise, find if a subset sums to total_sum/2.
options.B: Use 1D DP: dp[sum] = whether sum can be achieved.
options.C: Use DFS with pruning.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is the subset sum problem. Check if total_sum is even; if not, false. Target = total_sum/2. Then use DP: dp[0] = true; for each num, iterate from target down to num: dp[i] = dp[i] or dp[i-num]. Return dp[target]. O(n * target) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Maximum Product of Three Numbers
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, find three numbers whose product is maximum and return the maximum product.

options.A: Sort the array and return max(nums[n-1]*nums[n-2]*nums[n-3], nums[0]*nums[1]*nums[n-1]).
options.B: Find the three largest and two smallest numbers in one pass.
options.C: Use a min-heap and max-heap.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The maximum product can be either the product of the three largest numbers, or the product of the two smallest (most negative) numbers and the largest number (if the two negatives make a positive). Sorting is O(n log n), single-pass is O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Find the Duplicate Number
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums containing n+1 integers where each integer is in the range [1, n] inclusive. There is only one duplicate number, return the duplicate number. You cannot modify the array and must use constant extra space.

options.A: Sort the array and look for duplicate (modifies array).
options.B: Use Floyd's cycle detection (treating index jumps as pointers).
options.C: Use binary search on the value range (1 to n).
options.D: Both B and C work without modifying the array.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B (Floyd's) treats the array as a linked list where value = next index. Since there's a duplicate, there is a cycle. Find the cycle start. Option C (binary search) counts how many numbers are <= mid; if count > mid, the duplicate is in the left half. Both are O(n log n) or O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Find All Duplicates in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums of length n where all integers are in the range [1, n] and each integer appears once or twice, return an array of all the integers that appear twice.

options.A: Use a hash set to track seen numbers.
options.B: Mark visited indices by negating the value at nums[abs(num)-1].
options.C: Sort the array and find adjacent equals.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B uses O(1) extra space. For each num, compute index = abs(num)-1. If nums[index] is positive, negate it. If it's already negative, we have seen it before, so add abs(num) to the result. This works because values are in [1, n]. [citation:3]

questions.title: Merge Sorted Array (In-place)
questions.problem_statement: You are given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, sorted in non-decreasing order, and two integers m and n representing the number of elements in nums1 and nums2 respectively. Merge nums2 into nums1 so that the merged array is also sorted in-place.

options.A: Use a new array and merge, then copy back (O(m+n) space).
options.B: Merge from the end: set pointers at m-1, n-1, and m+n-1.
options.C: Insert nums2 elements into nums1 using binary search.
options.D: Both B is optimal for in-place.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Since nums1 has extra space at the end, we can merge from the back to avoid overwriting. Compare nums1[i] and nums2[j], place the larger at nums1[k] and decrement the respective pointer. This is O(m+n) time and O(1) extra space. [citation:1]

questions.title: Maximum Average Subarray I
questions.problem_statement: You are given an integer array nums consisting of n elements, and an integer k. Find a contiguous subarray of length k that has the maximum average value and return the maximum average.

options.A: Calculate sum of each subarray using nested loops (O(n*k)).
options.B: Use fixed-size sliding window: maintain window sum, slide, track max.
options.C: Use prefix sums to calculate subarray sums in O(1).
options.D: Both B and C are O(n).

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sliding window (Option B) is simpler: compute sum of first k elements, then for i from k to n: sum += nums[i] - nums[i-k], update max_sum. Prefix sums (Option C) also works: precompute prefix sums, then sum = prefix[i] - prefix[i-k]. Both O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Subarray Product Less Than K
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of positive integers nums and an integer k, return the number of contiguous subarrays where the product of all elements in the subarray is strictly less than k.

options.A: Generate all subarrays (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use sliding window: expand right, shrink left until product < k, then add (right-left+1) subarrays ending at right.
options.C: Use binary search on prefix logs to count subarrays with product < k.
options.D: Both B and C are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The sliding window solution is O(n). For each right index, multiply product by nums[right]. While product >= k and left <= right, divide product by nums[left] and increment left. Then add (right-left+1) to the count (these are all subarrays ending at right that satisfy the condition). [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
questions.problem_statement: You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character and change it to any other uppercase English character. Perform at most k operations. Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter after performing the operations.

options.A: Use sliding window: maintain a frequency array, track max frequency in the window.
options.B: While window_length - max_freq > k, shrink the window from the left.
options.C: Track the maximum window length that satisfies window_length - max_freq <= k.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The sliding window approach: expand right, update frequencies, and track max_count (most frequent character in the window). If window_size - max_count > k, we need more than k replacements, so shrink from left. The answer is the maximum window_size encountered. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Max Consecutive Ones III
questions.problem_statement: Given a binary array nums and an integer k, return the maximum number of consecutive 1's in the array if you can flip at most k 0's to 1's.

options.A: Use sliding window: count zeros in the window; if zeros > k, shrink from left.
options.B: Use two pointers to maintain a window with at most k zeros.
options.C: Use binary search on the answer.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The sliding window O(n) solution: expand the right pointer. If nums[right] == 0, increment zero_count. While zero_count > k, move left pointer and decrement zero_count if nums[left] is 0. Update max_len = max(max_len, right-left+1). Option C with binary search also works but is O(n log n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Fruit Into Baskets
questions.problem_statement: You are visiting a farm with a row of fruit trees. You have two baskets, and each basket can hold only one type of fruit. You want to collect as many fruits as possible, but you cannot skip any trees once you start. Return the maximum number of fruits you can collect.

options.A: This is equivalent to the longest substring with at most 2 distinct characters.
options.B: Use sliding window with a hash map to count fruit types.
options.C: When the number of distinct fruits exceeds 2, shrink from left until only 2 remain.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is exactly the 'longest substring with at most 2 distinct characters' problem. Use a sliding window and a hash map (or dictionary) to track counts. When the map size > 2, shrink the window until size <= 2. Keep track of the maximum window length. [citation:3]

questions.title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of positive integers nums and a target sum s, return the minimal length of a contiguous subarray whose sum is greater than or equal to s. If there is none, return 0.

options.A: Use nested loops to check all subarrays (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use sliding window: expand right, while sum >= s, update min_len, shrink left.
options.C: Use binary search on the subarray length with prefix sums.
options.D: Both B and C are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sliding window is O(n): expand right, add to sum. While sum >= target, update min_len, then subtract nums[left] and increment left. This works because all numbers are positive. Binary search on length (O(n log n)) also works but is less efficient. [citation:3]

questions.title: Permutation in String
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings s1 and s2, return true if s2 contains a permutation of s1. In other words, return true if there is a substring in s2 that is a permutation of s1.

options.A: Generate all permutations of s1 and check if they appear in s2 (O(n!)).
options.B: Use a fixed-size sliding window of length len(s1) and compare character counts.
options.C: Use a hash map for s1's frequency and a sliding window to update counts.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The optimal solution uses a sliding window of length len(s1). Maintain a count array for s1 and for the current window. Slide the window across s2, updating the window's frequency by adding the new character and removing the old one. Compare the frequency arrays. O(len(s2)) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Minimum Window Substring
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings s and t, return the minimum window in s which will contain all the characters in t. If there is no such window, return an empty string.

options.A: For each starting index, expand until all characters are found (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use sliding window with two pointers: expand right, when all chars found, shrink left to minimize.
options.C: Use a hash map to store needed counts and a counter for matched chars.
options.D: Both B and C are standard.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The standard O(n) sliding window solution: Maintain a frequency map for t and a map for the current window. Use a 'formed' counter to track how many characters are satisfied. Expand right. When all characters are satisfied, shrink left to find the smallest window, updating the result accordingly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Sliding Window Maximum
questions.problem_statement: You are given an array of integers nums and an integer k. For each sliding window of size k moving from left to right, return the maximum element in the window.

options.A: For each window, find the max using a loop (O(n*k)).
options.B: Use a max-heap (priority queue) with lazy deletion (O(n log k)).
options.C: Use a monotonic deque (decreasing order) to store indices (O(n)).

questions.detailed_reasoning: The monotonic deque solution is O(n). Maintain a deque that stores indices of elements in decreasing order. Before adding a new element, remove indices from the back where nums[index] <= nums[new]. Also remove indices that are out of the window from the front. The front of the deque is always the maximum in the current window. [citation:3]

questions.title: Reverse String
questions.problem_statement: Write a function that reverses a string. The input string is given as an array of characters s. You must do this by modifying the input array in-place with O(1) extra memory.

options.A: Create a new array and copy in reverse (O(n) space).
options.B: Use two pointers: left=0, right=len-1; swap and move inward.
options.C: Reverse using recursion.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The two-pointer approach is the most efficient: swap the first and last characters, then move the pointers inward until they meet. This is O(n) time and O(1) space. Recursion also works but uses O(n) stack space. [citation:1]

questions.title: Reverse Words in a String
questions.problem_statement: Given an input string s, reverse the order of the words. A word is defined as a sequence of non-space characters. The words in s are separated by at least one space. Return a string of the words in reverse order, separated by a single space.

options.A: Split the string by space, reverse the list, join with space.
options.B: Reverse the entire string, then reverse each word.
options.C: Use a stack to push words and pop.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A is the simplest in Python/JavaScript. Option B is the traditional in-place approach for languages with mutable strings: reverse the whole string, then reverse each word. Option C works with a stack but uses extra space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Common Prefix
questions.problem_statement: Write a function to find the longest common prefix string amongst an array of strings. If there is no common prefix, return an empty string.

options.A: Take the first string as prefix; for each string, reduce the prefix until it matches.
options.B: Sort the strings and compare the first and last string.
options.C: Use vertical scanning: compare characters at the same index across all strings.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All approaches work. Horizontal scanning (A) is O(S) where S is the sum of all characters. Sorting and comparing first and last (B) is O(n log n + m) where m is the length of the shortest string. Vertical scanning (C) is also O(S). [citation:8]

questions.title: String to Integer (atoi)
questions.problem_statement: Implement the myAtoi(string s) function, which converts a string to a 32-bit signed integer. The algorithm should handle leading whitespace, optional sign, overflow, and non-digit characters after the number.

options.A: Use the built-in int() function.
options.B: Manual parsing: skip whitespace, read sign, read digits, handle overflow with INT_MAX/10 checks.
options.C: Use regular expressions to extract the number.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Manual parsing is the expected solution. Steps: 1) Skip leading spaces. 2) Check sign. 3) Read digits until a non-digit or end. 4) Build result but check overflow: if result > INT_MAX/10, or result == INT_MAX/10 and digit > 7 (for positive), then clamp. Return the clamped value. [citation:3]

questions.title: Implement strStr() (Index of first occurrence)
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings haystack and needle, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if needle is not part of haystack.

options.A: Use Python's find() method.
options.B: Use nested loops: for each i, compare substring.
options.C: Use the KMP algorithm (O(m+n)).
options.D: Both B and C are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: For interviews, nested loops (O(m*n)) are acceptable and simpler. The optimal solution is KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) with O(m+n) time, but it's more complex to implement. The built-in method (A) is not allowed in an implementation problem. [citation:8]

questions.title: Valid Number
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, return whether s is a valid number. A valid number can be an integer, decimal, or scientific notation (e.g., '2e10').

options.A: Use a finite state machine (DFA).
options.B: Try converting to float and catch exception (not allowed in an implementation problem).
options.C: Use regular expressions.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The DFA approach is the standard solution for this problem. Define states for: sign, digits before dot, dot, digits after dot, exponent sign, exponent digits. Process each character and transition states. At the end, you must be in a valid end state (digit, digits after dot, etc.). [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest Palindromic Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest subsequence that is a palindrome. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from the string by deleting some characters without changing the order.

options.A: Use 2D DP: if s[i]==s[j], dp[i][j] = 2 + dp[i+1][j-1]; else dp[i][j] = max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
options.B: Reverse the string and find the longest common subsequence.
options.C: Use recursion with memoization.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The DP recurrence is correct: dp[i][j] is the LPS length for s[i..j]. For i==j, it's 1. For i > j, it's 0. If characters match, 2 + dp[i+1][j-1]; else take the max of the two subproblems. Option B works because the LPS of a string is the LCS of the string and its reverse. [citation:3]

questions.title: Palindromic Substrings
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, return the number of palindromic substrings in it. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters. Substrings with different start or end indices are considered different.

options.A: Expand around center for each possible center (2n-1 centers).
options.B: Use DP with a 2D table.
options.C: Use Manacher's algorithm for O(n).

questions.detailed_reasoning: Expand around center is O(n^2) and simpler. For each center (each character and between characters), expand outward while characters match and count. DP is also O(n^2) but uses O(n^2) space. Manacher's algorithm is O(n) but much more complex. For interviews, expand around center is sufficient. [citation:3]

questions.title: Count and Say
questions.problem_statement: The count-and-say sequence is the sequence of integers: 1, 11, 21, 1211, 111221, ... Given an integer n, return the n-th term of the sequence.

options.A: Use recursion: countAndSay(n) = say(countAndSay(n-1)).
options.B: Use iteration: start with '1', for each step, group consecutive characters and say count+digit.
options.C: Use a regex to find groups of repeated characters.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Iterative is straightforward: start with '1'. For the next term, traverse the string, count the number of repeated digits, and append count and digit to the result. Recursion works similarly. Both are O(n * 2^n) in length, which grows quickly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Multiply Strings
questions.problem_statement: Given two non-negative integers num1 and num2 represented as strings, return the product of num1 and num2, also represented as a string. You cannot use any built-in BigInteger library or convert the inputs to integers directly.

options.A: Simulate the multiplication algorithm: for each digit in num2, multiply with num1 and accumulate with shifts.
options.B: Use an array to store intermediate products and handle carries at the end.
options.C: Use Karatsuba algorithm for better time complexity.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Standard approach: create an array result of size m+n. For i from m-1 down to 0, for j from n-1 down to 0, multiply digits, add to result[i+j+1]. Then handle carries: result[i] += result[i+1]/10, result[i+1] %= 10. Then convert to string, skipping leading zeros. O(m*n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Integer to Roman
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer, convert it to a Roman numeral. Roman numerals are represented by seven symbols: I(1), V(5), X(10), L(50), C(100), D(500), M(1000).

options.A: Use a greedy algorithm with arrays of values and corresponding symbols.
options.B: Use recursion: for each value, subtract the largest possible roman value.
options.C: Use a list of tuples (value, symbol) for all special cases (e.g., 4, 9, 40, 90, 400, 900).

questions.detailed_reasoning: The greedy approach works because Roman numerals are standard. Create arrays: values = [1000,900,500,400,100,90,50,40,10,9,5,4,1] and corresponding symbols. For each value, while num >= value, append symbol and subtract value. This covers all cases including subtraction rules. [citation:3]

questions.title: Roman to Integer
questions.problem_statement: Given a Roman numeral string, convert it to an integer.

options.A: Use a map to store symbol values. Iterate: if current value < next value, subtract; else add.
options.B: Replace all subtraction cases (e.g., 'IV' -> 'IIII') and sum.
options.C: Use recursion to parse the string.

questions.detailed_reasoning: One-pass O(n) solution: map each character to its value. Iterate i from 0 to n-1. If i < n-1 and value[s[i]] < value[s[i+1]], subtract; otherwise add. This handles cases like 'IV' (1 < 5, so subtract) correctly. [citation:8]

questions.title: Simplify Path
questions.problem_statement: Given a string path, which is an absolute Unix-style path, simplify it to a canonical path. The canonical path should start with a single '/', end without a trailing '/', and remove any '.' or '..' components.

options.A: Split the path by '/', use a stack to handle directory names: push if name not '.' or '..', pop if '..' and stack not empty.
options.B: Use a deque to process components.
options.C: Use string manipulation to replace patterns.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The stack approach is standard: split the path by '/' to get components. For each component: skip if empty or '.'; if '..', pop from stack if not empty; otherwise, push the component. Finally, join the stack with '/' and ensure it starts with '/'. [citation:3]

questions.title: Zigzag Conversion
questions.problem_statement: The string 'PAYPALISHIRING' is written in a zigzag pattern on a given number of rows. Convert the string to a new string that reads row by row.

options.A: Create an array of strings for each row. Simulate the zigzag: go down, then up.
options.B: Calculate the pattern mathematically: characters in row 0 are at indices 2*(numRows-1)*k, etc.
options.C: Use a 2D matrix and fill positions.
options.D: Both A and B are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option A is simpler: iterate through the string, maintain current row and direction (down/up). Append each character to the corresponding row string. At the end, concatenate all rows. O(n) time. Option B is more mathematical and avoids string appends but is harder to implement. [citation:3]

questions.title: Add Binary
questions.problem_statement: Given two binary strings a and b, return their sum as a binary string.

options.A: Convert to integers, add, and convert back (not allowed for large strings).
options.B: Simulate binary addition from right to left with carry.
options.C: Use bit manipulation with Python's bin() after converting to int.
options.D: Both B is the correct manual approach.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Manual addition: initialize result = [], carry = 0, i = len(a)-1, j = len(b)-1. While i >=0 or j >=0 or carry: sum = carry + (a[i] if i>=0 else 0) + (b[j] if j>=0 else 0), result.append(str(sum%2)), carry = sum//2. Then reverse and join. O(max(m,n)) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Valid Palindrome II
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, return true if the string can be a palindrome after deleting at most one character from it.

options.A: Use two pointers; when mismatch occurs, check if s[left+1:right] or s[left:right-1] is a palindrome.
options.B: Use recursion with a flag indicating whether a deletion has been used.
options.C: Try deleting each character and check palindrome (O(n^2)).
options.D: Both A and B are efficient approaches.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The O(n) solution: two pointers from both ends. When s[left] != s[right], check if the substring s[left+1:right] or s[left:right-1] is a palindrome. If either is true, return true. This effectively skips one character. [citation:3]

questions.title: Backspace String Compare
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings s and t, return true if they are equal when both are typed into empty text editors. '#' means a backspace character.

options.A: Use a stack to process both strings and compare.
options.B: Use two pointers from the end: skip characters that are backspaced.
options.C: Build the final string using a list as a stack.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B is O(n) time and O(1) space. Process from the end: maintain a skip count. For each character, if '#', increment skip; else if skip > 0, skip this char; else, compare the current characters from s and t. This avoids building the strings. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String (KMP)
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings haystack and needle, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if needle is not part of haystack.

options.A: Use Python's find() method.
options.B: Use the KMP algorithm to achieve O(m+n) time.
options.C: Use a rolling hash (Rabin-Karp) for O(m+n) average time.
options.D: Both B and C are optimal.

questions.detailed_reasoning: KMP precomputes a prefix function (LPS array) for the needle, then scans the haystack without backtracking. Rabin-Karp uses a hash function to compare substrings in O(1). Both are efficient and useful to know for interviews. [citation:3]

questions.title: Excel Sheet Column Title
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer columnNumber, return its corresponding column title as it appears in an Excel sheet (1 -> A, 2 -> B, ..., 26 -> Z, 27 -> AA).

options.A: Convert the number to base 26, but with digits 1-26 instead of 0-25.
options.B: While columnNumber > 0: subtract 1, columnNumber % 26 gets the letter, columnNumber //= 26.
options.C: Use a recursive approach to build the string.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The tricky part is that there is no zero digit. So we do: n--; letter = chr('A' + n % 26); n //= 26. This maps 1-26 to A-Z correctly. Then reverse the collected letters. [citation:3]

questions.title: Excel Sheet Column Number
questions.problem_statement: Given a string columnTitle that represents the column title as it appears in an Excel sheet, return its corresponding column number.

options.A: For each character, result = result * 26 + (char - 'A' + 1).
options.B: Use a recursive function to compute the value.
options.C: Use a map to store letter values.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is similar to converting base 26 with digits 1-26. Iterate through the string: result = result * 26 + (ord(c) - ord('A') + 1). This works because 'A' is 1, 'AA' = 26*1 + 1 = 27. [citation:3]

questions.title: Factorial Trailing Zeroes
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return the number of trailing zeroes in n! (n factorial).

options.A: Compute n! and count trailing zeros (overflows quickly).
options.B: Count the number of factors of 5 (since 2s are abundant).
options.C: Use the formula: count = n/5 + n/25 + n/125 + ...
options.D: Both B and C describe the correct approach.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Trailing zeros come from factors of 10 = 2 * 5. There are always more factors of 2 than 5, so count factors of 5. Count = floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ... while the divisor <= n. This gives O(log n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Happy Number
questions.problem_statement: Write an algorithm to determine if a number n is happy. A happy number is replaced by the sum of the squares of its digits repeatedly until it equals 1 or loops endlessly in a cycle.

options.A: Use a hash set to detect cycles.
options.B: Use Floyd's cycle detection (slow and fast pointers) on the digit square sum function.
options.C: If the number reaches 1, return true; if it enters a cycle (like 4 -> 16 -> 37 -> ...), return false.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The cycle detection approach is standard: compute the sum of squares of digits repeatedly. Use a set to track seen numbers. If 1 is reached, return true. If a number repeats, false. Floyd's algorithm (Option B) uses O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Plus One
questions.problem_statement: You are given a large integer represented as an integer array digits, where each digits[i] is the i-th digit of the integer. Add one to the integer and return the resulting array.

options.A: Convert to integer, add one, and convert back (risk of overflow).
options.B: Simulate addition from right to left with carry.
options.C: If all digits are 9, return a new array with a leading 1 followed by zeros.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Simulate addition: iterate from the end; add one to the last digit. If it becomes 10, set it to 0 and continue the carry. If we finish the loop with carry=1, insert 1 at the beginning. This is O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Pow(x, n)
questions.problem_statement: Implement pow(x, n), which calculates x raised to the power n (i.e., x^n).

options.A: Use a loop to multiply x, n times (O(n)).
options.B: Use binary exponentiation (fast power) with recursion or iteration (O(log n)).
options.C: Handle negative exponents by computing 1 / pow(x, -n).

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary exponentiation: while n > 0, if n is odd, multiply result by x; then x = x * x; n //= 2. For negative n, compute pow(x, -n) and take reciprocal. Handle edge cases: n = 0 returns 1, x = 0 returns 0, negative n with x=0. [citation:3]

questions.title: Sqrt(x) (Binary Search)
questions.problem_statement: Given a non-negative integer x, return the square root of x rounded down to the nearest integer. The returned integer should be the greatest integer less than or equal to the square root of x.

options.A: Use Python's math.isqrt(x).
options.B: Use binary search: find the largest mid such that mid*mid <= x.
options.C: Use Newton's method: guess = (guess + x/guess) / 2.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Binary search is the standard interview solution. Search between 1 and x. While left <= right, check mid = left + (right-left)/2. If mid*mid == x, return mid; else if mid*mid < x, left = mid+1; else right = mid-1. After the loop, return right (the floor). [citation:3]

questions.title: Divide Two Integers
questions.problem_statement: Given two integers dividend and divisor, divide two integers without using multiplication, division, and mod operator. Return the quotient after truncating toward zero.

options.A: Repeatedly subtract the divisor from the dividend (O(n)).
options.B: Use binary search on the quotient.
options.C: Use bit manipulation: double the divisor until it's too big, then subtract.
options.D: Both B and C are efficient approaches.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The bit manipulation approach: shifting left is doubling. Find the largest multiple that fits by shifting the divisor left until it exceeds the dividend. Then subtract and continue. This reduces complexity to O(log n). Handle overflow and sign carefully. [citation:3]

questions.title: Fraction to Recurring Decimal
questions.problem_statement: Given two integers numerator and denominator, return the fraction in string format. If the fractional part is repeating, enclose the repeating part in parentheses.

options.A: Perform long division and use a hash map to detect repeating remainders.
options.B: Handle sign, integer part, then fractional part with a map from remainder to position.
options.C: Use a set to detect cycles.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Standard approach: process integer part first. Then for the fractional part, keep a hash map from remainder to the index where it appeared. When a remainder repeats, the digits from that index to the current position are the repeating part. Insert parentheses accordingly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Count Primes
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return the number of prime numbers that are strictly less than n.

options.A: Check each number for primality (O(n sqrt(n))).
options.B: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes (O(n log log n)).
options.C: Use a segmented sieve for large n.
options.D: Both B is the standard efficient approach.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sieve of Eratosthenes: create a boolean array of size n, initially all true. For i from 2 to sqrt(n), if prime[i] is true, mark multiples from i*i as false. Finally, count true values. This runs in O(n log log n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Ugly Number
questions.problem_statement: An ugly number is a positive integer whose prime factors are limited to 2, 3, and 5. Given an integer n, return true if n is an ugly number.

options.A: Divide n by 2, 3, 5 repeatedly until no longer divisible; if result == 1, return true.
options.B: Check if n has any prime factor other than 2, 3, 5.
options.C: Use recursion to divide n by 2, 3, or 5.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The iterative approach: while n % 2 == 0, n//=2; while n % 3 == 0, n//=3; while n % 5 == 0, n//=5. After that, if n == 1, it's ugly; otherwise, it has some other prime factor. O(log n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Ugly Number II
questions.problem_statement: Find the n-th ugly number. Ugly numbers are positive numbers whose prime factors are only 2, 3, and 5. The first ugly number is 1.

options.A: Generate numbers and check if they are ugly (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use three pointers (p2, p3, p5) to generate the next ugly number from multiples of existing ones.
options.C: Use a min-heap to generate the sequence.
options.D: Both B and C are standard.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The three-pointer DP approach is classic: maintain dp[i] for the i-th ugly number. p2, p3, p5 are pointers tracking the last ugly number used for multiplication. Next ugly = min(dp[p2]*2, dp[p3]*3, dp[p5]*5). Increment the pointer(s) that produced the minimum. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Missing Number (Sum Formula)
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.

options.A: Compute expected_sum = n*(n+1)//2, actual_sum = sum(nums), missing = expected_sum - actual_sum.
options.B: Use XOR (already covered).
options.C: Sort the array and find the gap.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The sum formula is straightforward: the sum of 0 to n is n*(n+1)/2. Subtract the sum of the array to get the missing number. However, be cautious of integer overflow in some languages; XOR avoids overflow. [citation:3]

questions.title: Add Digits
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer num, repeatedly add all its digits until the result has only one digit, and return it.

options.A: Use a loop: while num >= 10, sum digits.
options.B: Use the digital root formula: if num == 0 return 0; else return 1 + (num-1) % 9.
options.C: Use recursion.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The digital root formula is O(1): for a non-zero number, the digital root is 1 + (num-1) % 9. This is derived from the property that a number is congruent to the sum of its digits modulo 9. [citation:3]

questions.title: Rectangle Overlap
questions.problem_statement: Given two rectangles represented by their bottom-left and top-right corners, determine if the rectangles overlap. Overlap means they share at least some area; touching at edges or corners does not count as overlapping.

options.A: Check if one rectangle is to the left, right, above, or below the other.
options.B: Use the principle: two rectangles do NOT overlap if one is completely to the left, right, above, or below of the other.
options.C: Use the overlap of intervals on the x-axis and y-axis.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Overlap on both axes: overlap_x = max(0, min(rec1.x2, rec2.x2) - max(rec1.x1, rec1.x1)). If overlap_x > 0 and overlap_y > 0, they overlap. For non-overlap: check rec1.x2 <= rec2.x1 or rec2.x2 <= rec1.x1 or rec1.y2 <= rec2.y1 or rec2.y2 <= rec1.y1. [citation:8]

questions.title: Reverse Integer
questions.problem_statement: Given a signed 32-bit integer x, return x with its digits reversed. If reversing x causes the value to go outside the signed 32-bit integer range [-2^31, 2^31-1], return 0.

options.A: Convert to string, reverse, convert back, check overflow.
options.B: Pop digits: result = result*10 + digit. Check overflow before adding.
options.C: Use Python's integer (unbounded) and clamp at the end.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Manual method: while x != 0, digit = x % 10, x //= 10. Check if result > INT_MAX/10 or (result == INT_MAX/10 and digit > 7) for positive; similarly for negative. If overflow, return 0. [citation:1]

questions.title: Palindrome Number
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer x, return true if x is a palindrome. An integer is a palindrome when it reads the same backward as forward. Do not convert the integer to a string.

options.A: Convert to string and check (not allowed).
options.B: Reverse half of the number: revert = revert*10 + x%10; x //=10. Stop when revert >= x.
options.C: Compare the first and last digits by extracting them.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Reverse half the number: while x > reverted, reverted = reverted*10 + x%10; x //=10. Then palindrome if x == reverted or x == reverted//10 (for odd lengths). This avoids overflow and string conversion. [citation:3]

questions.title: Fizz Buzz
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return a list of strings where: for multiples of 3 output 'Fizz', for multiples of 5 output 'Buzz', for multiples of both output 'FizzBuzz', otherwise the number as a string.

options.A: Use modulo operators in a loop.
options.B: Use string concatenation: start empty, add 'Fizz' if divisible by 3, add 'Buzz' if divisible by 5, else add the number.
options.C: Use a hash map for conditions to make it extensible.

questions.detailed_reasoning: All approaches work. The string concatenation method is elegant: if i%3==0: output='Fizz'; if i%5==0: output+='Buzz'; if output=='': output=str(i). This avoids checking the combination case explicitly. [citation:3]

questions.title: Sum of Two Integers
questions.problem_statement: Given two integers a and b, return the sum of the two integers without using the operators + and -.

options.A: Use bit manipulation: XOR for sum without carry, AND for carry, shift left, repeat until carry is 0.
options.B: Use a while loop: while b != 0, carry = a & b, a = a ^ b, b = carry << 1; return a.
options.C: Use recursion.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The core is: a ^ b gives the sum without carry; (a & b) << 1 gives the carry. Keep adding the carry to the sum until no carry remains. This is the standard algorithm. [citation:3]

questions.title: Power of Two
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return true if it is a power of two. A power of two has exactly one '1' bit in its binary representation.

options.A: Check if n > 0 and (n & (n-1)) == 0.
options.B: Count the number of set bits; if it's 1, return true.
options.C: Use the log2 function and check if it's an integer.

questions.detailed_reasoning: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. If the result is 0 and n > 0, it is a power of two. This is O(1). Option B (counting bits) also works. [citation:3]

questions.title: Power of Three
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return true if it is a power of three. Follow up: Could you solve it without loops or recursion?

options.A: Divide by 3 repeatedly until not divisible; if result == 1, return true.
options.B: Check if n > 0 and 1162261467 % n == 0 (since 3^19 is the largest power of 3 within 32-bit int).
options.C: Use the log3 function: abs(log10(n)/log10(3) - round(...)) < epsilon.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The constant method (Option B) is clever: the largest 32-bit power of 3 is 1162261467. Any power of 3 will divide this number. This gives O(1) with no loops. [citation:3]

questions.title: Hamming Distance
questions.problem_statement: The Hamming distance between two integers is the number of positions at which the corresponding bits are different. Given two integers x and y, return the Hamming distance.

options.A: Compute xor = x ^ y, then count the number of '1' bits in xor.
options.B: Use built-in bit_count() or bin(xor).count('1').
options.C: Use a loop to count bits: while xor: count++, xor &= xor-1.

questions.detailed_reasoning: xor gives 1s where bits differ. Then count the set bits. The method xor & (xor-1) clears the lowest set bit each time, giving O(number of set bits) complexity. [citation:3]

questions.title: Counting Bits
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return an array ans of length n+1 such that ans[i] is the number of '1' bits in the binary representation of i.

options.A: For each i, count bits using a loop (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use DP: ans[i] = ans[i >> 1] + (i & 1).
options.C: Use DP: ans[i] = ans[i & (i-1)] + 1.

questions.detailed_reasoning: DP approach O(n): ans[i] = ans[i//2] + (i%2). Or ans[i] = ans[i & (i-1)] + 1, which uses the trick that i & (i-1) removes the lowest set bit. Both are O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Missing Number (XOR)
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.

options.A: XOR all numbers from 0 to n and XOR with all elements in the array.
options.B: Use sum formula.
options.C: Use sorting.

questions.detailed_reasoning: XOR: missing = 0; for i in range(len(nums)+1): missing ^= i; for num in nums: missing ^= num; return missing. This cancels out all numbers that appear twice, leaving the missing one. [citation:3]

questions.title: Find the Difference
questions.problem_statement: You are given two strings s and t where t is generated by shuffling s and adding one extra character at a random position. Return the added character.

options.A: Use a hash map to count characters.
options.B: XOR all characters of s and t together; the result is the extra character.
options.C: Sort both strings and find the first mismatch.

questions.detailed_reasoning: XOR has the property that a XOR a = 0. XOR all characters in s and t together. The characters that appear twice cancel out, leaving the character that appears only once (the extra one). O(n) time, O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return the maximum result of nums[i] XOR nums[j] where 0 <= i <= j < n.

options.A: Use nested loops (O(n^2)).
options.B: Use a trie (binary trie) to store the bits of numbers and query for maximum XOR.
options.C: Use the prefix XOR approach.
options.D: Both B is the optimal solution.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Insert all numbers into a binary trie (bits from MSB to LSB). For each number, traverse the trie preferring the opposite bit to maximize XOR. This yields O(32*n) = O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Single Number II
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array where every element appears three times except for one, which appears once. Find that single element. Your algorithm should have O(n) time and O(1) space.

options.A: Use a hash map to count frequencies.
options.B: Use bitwise counting: for each bit position, count how many numbers have that bit set; if count % 3 == 1, set the bit in the result.
options.C: Use two variables: ones and twos to track bits seen once and twice.
options.D: Both B and C are valid.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Option B: for each bit position, sum the bits modulo 3. If the remainder is 1, that bit is set in the single number. Option C uses `ones ^= num; twos |= ones & ...` to simulate base-3 addition. Both are O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Bitwise AND of Numbers Range
questions.problem_statement: Given two integers left and right that represent the range [left, right], return the bitwise AND of all numbers in this range, inclusive.

options.A: Iterate from left to right and perform AND (O(n) but n can be large).
options.B: Find the common prefix of the binary representations of left and right.
options.C: While left < right, right &= right-1; return left & right.
options.D: Both B and C are efficient.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The result is the common prefix. Option C: repeatedly clear the lowest set bit of right until left <= right. This works because ANDing all numbers from left to right will clear all bits where any number in the range has a 0. O(log n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Two Sum (Sorted Array, Two Pointers)
questions.problem_statement: Given a 1-indexed sorted array of integers, find two numbers that sum to a target. Return their indices.

options.A: Use binary search for each element (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use two pointers from left and right.
options.C: Use a hash map.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Since the array is sorted, the two-pointer approach is optimal: left=0, right=n-1. If sum > target, right--; if sum < target, left++; else return. This is O(n) and O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Container With Most Water (Two Pointers)
questions.problem_statement: Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water.

options.A: Two pointers: start at ends, move the pointer with the smaller height inward.
options.B: Brute force: check all pairs.
options.C: Use a monotonic stack.
options.D: Both A is the standard solution.

questions.detailed_reasoning: The area is limited by the shorter line. By moving the pointer with the smaller height, we might increase the height and potentially get a larger area, even though the width decreases. O(n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Trapping Rain Water (Two Pointers)
questions.problem_statement: Given elevation map, compute how much water it can trap.

options.A: For each bar, find left max and right max (O(n) time, O(n) space).
options.B: Two pointers: left=0, right=n-1, left_max=0, right_max=0.
options.C: Use a stack to maintain decreasing heights.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Two-pointer O(n) time, O(1) space: move the pointer with the smaller max inward, as the water trapped at that pointer is determined by the smaller of left_max and right_max minus the height. This is optimal. [citation:3]

questions.title: 3Sum (Two Pointers after sorting)
questions.problem_statement: Find all triplets that sum to zero.

options.A: Sort array, fix one element, use two pointers for the other two.
options.B: Use a hash set to store pairs.
options.C: Use three nested loops.
options.D: Both A is the standard O(n^2) solution.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sorting allows two-pointer technique for the pair sum, avoiding the O(n^3) brute force. Handle duplicates by skipping repeated values. O(n^2) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Valid Palindrome (Two Pointers)
questions.problem_statement: Check if a string is a palindrome after cleaning.

options.A: Use two pointers: left and right, skipping non-alphanumeric characters.
options.B: Reverse the string and compare.
options.C: Use a stack.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Two-pointer approach is O(n) time and O(1) space. Skip non-alphanumeric characters and compare lowercase versions. [citation:3]

questions.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array (Two Pointers)
questions.problem_statement: Remove duplicates in-place and return the new length.

options.A: Use two pointers: slow tracks last unique, fast scans.
options.B: Use a hash set.
options.C: Shift elements after removal.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Set slow=0; for fast from 1..n-1: if nums[fast] != nums[slow], slow++, nums[slow]=nums[fast]. Return slow+1. O(n) time, O(1) space. [citation:3]

questions.title: Move Zeroes (Two Pointers)
questions.problem_statement: Move all zeros to the end while preserving order.

options.A: Use two pointers: slow for non-zero position, fast to scan.
options.B: Count zeros and rebuild array.
options.C: Swap zeros with next non-zero.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Algorithm: slow=0; for fast in range(n): if nums[fast] != 0: nums[slow], nums[fast] = nums[fast], nums[slow]; slow++ . This maintains order. O(n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Two Sum Less Than K
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums and an integer k, return the maximum sum of a pair (i, j) such that i<j and nums[i] + nums[j] < k. If no such pair exists, return -1.

options.A: Sort the array and use two pointers.
options.B: Use brute force (O(n^2)).
options.C: Use a hash set.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sort the array. Use left=0, right=n-1. Initialize max_sum = -1. While left < right, sum = nums[left]+nums[right]. If sum < k, update max_sum, left++; else right--. This finds the maximum sum less than k. O(n log n). [citation:3]

questions.title: Squares of a Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, return an array of the squares of each number sorted in non-decreasing order.

options.A: Square each element and sort (O(n log n)).
options.B: Use two pointers: left=0, right=n-1; compare absolute values and fill result from the end.
options.C: Use a priority queue.

options.B: Initialize dp[0][j] as sum of s2[:j], dp[i][0] as sum of s1[:i].
options.C: Use 1D DP.

questions.detailed_reasoning: This is similar to edit distance but with deletion cost equal to ASCII value. The recurrence is straightforward. O(m*n) time. [citation:3]

questions.title: Longest String Chain (DP)
questions.problem_statement: Given a list of words, find the longest chain where each next word is the previous word with one character inserted anywhere.

options.A: Sort by length. DP: dp[word] = 1 + max(dp[predecessor]) for all predecessors.
options.B: Generate all predecessors by removing one character.
options.C: Use hash map to store dp.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Sort by length. Use a dictionary mapping word to max chain length. For each word, try removing each character to get a predecessor and update dp[word]. O(n * L^2) where L is word length. [citation:3]

questions.title: Largest Plus Sign (DP)
questions.problem_statement: In an N x N grid of 1s, some mines (0s). Find the largest plus sign (+ shape) of 1s.

options.A: DP: left[i][j], right[i][j], up[i][j], down[i][j].
options.B: For each cell, the plus size is min of the four directions.
options.C: Precompute running counts in four directions.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Precompute the number of consecutive 1s to the left, right, up, down for each cell. Then the plus sign order at (i,j) is min(left,right,up,down). O(n^2). [citation:3]

questions.title: Cherry Pickup (DP)
questions.problem_statement: From (0,0) to (n-1,n-1) and back to (0,0), picking cherries, max total.

options.A: Two paths from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1) simultaneously.
options.B: DP: dp[r1][c1][c2] (or steps s, r1, r2).
options.C: The number of steps determines the coordinates.

questions.detailed_reasoning: Instead of going back, consider two people walking from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1) simultaneously. State: steps, row1, row2. c1 = steps - row1, c2 = steps - row2. If (r1,c1) == (r2,c2), add grid[r1][c1]; else add both. [citation:3]

questions.title: Maximum Subarray Sum with Kadane's (Negative numbers)
questions.problem_statement: Find the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray (there is at least one number).

options.A: current = max(num, current+num); max_sum = max(max_sum, current).
options.B: Initialize current = nums[0], max_sum = nums[0].
options.C: Works for all-negative arrays.
